The **Han dynasty**[[a]](#cite_note-5) (202 BC – 220 AD) was an [imperial dynasty](/wiki/Dynasties_of_China "Dynasties of China") of [China](/wiki/China "China") established by [Liu Bang](/wiki/Liu_Bang "Liu Bang"), preceded by the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BC) and a warring [interregnum](/wiki/Interregnum "Interregnum") known as the [Chu–Han Contention](/wiki/Chu%E2%80%93Han_Contention "Chu–Han Contention") (206–202 BC), and succeeded by the [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms") period (220–280 AD). It was also briefly interrupted by the short-lived [Xin dynasty](/wiki/Xin_dynasty "Xin dynasty") (9–23 AD) established by the [usurping](/wiki/Usurp "Usurp") [regent](/wiki/Regent "Regent") [Wang Mang](/wiki/Wang_Mang "Wang Mang"), and is thus separated into two periods — the [Western Han](#Western_Han_(202_BC_–_9_AD)) (202 BC – 9 AD) and the [Eastern Han](#Eastern_Han_(25–220_AD)) (25–220 AD), named after the respective locations of the capitals [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an") and [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang"). Considered a prominent [golden age](/wiki/Golden_ages_of_China "Golden ages of China") in [Chinese history](/wiki/Chinese_history "Chinese history"), the Han dynasty had long-lasting impact on both the [political uniformity](/wiki/Chinese_uniformity "Chinese uniformity") and [cultural identity](/wiki/Cultural_identity "Cultural identity") of the [Chinese nation](/wiki/Chinese_nation "Chinese nation") in later periods.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou200334-6) The [titular](/wiki/Titular_nation "Titular nation") [ethnic majority](/wiki/Ethnic_majority "Ethnic majority") of modern [Chinese people](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people") refer to themselves as the "[Han people](/wiki/Han_people "Han people")", while [spoken](/wiki/Spoken_Chinese "Spoken Chinese") and [written Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese") are referred to as the "[Han language](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language")" and "[Han characters](/wiki/Han_characters "Han characters")", respectively.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchaefer2008279-7)

The [Han emperors](/wiki/List_of_emperors_of_the_Han_dynasty "List of emperors of the Han dynasty") were at the pinnacle of [contemporary society and culture](/wiki/Society_and_culture_of_the_Han_dynasty "Society and culture of the Han dynasty"), presiding over the [imperial government](/wiki/Government_of_the_Han_dynasty "Government of the Han dynasty") but sharing power with both the [nobility](/wiki/Chinese_nobility "Chinese nobility") and appointed [scholar-official](/wiki/Scholar-official "Scholar-official") [bureaucrats](/wiki/Mandarin_(bureaucrat) "Mandarin (bureaucrat)"), who came largely from the [classically](/wiki/Four_Books_and_Five_Classics "Four Books and Five Classics") educated [gentry class](/wiki/Landed_gentry_in_China "Landed gentry in China"). The Han Empire initially adopted an hybrid political system where the realm was divided into Qin-style [commandery](/wiki/Commandery_(China) "Commandery (China)")-[county](/wiki/County_(China) "County (China)") [administrative regions](/wiki/Administrative_region "Administrative region") directly controlled by the imperial [central government](/wiki/Central_government "Central government"), and semi-[autonomous](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division") *[fengjian](/wiki/Fengjian "Fengjian")* [kingdoms](/wiki/Kings_of_the_Han_dynasty "Kings of the Han dynasty") that were [self-governed](/wiki/Self-governance "Self-governance") by princely noblemen, reminiscent of the [feudal states](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_states "Ancient Chinese states") of the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") (c. 1050–256 BC). These kingdoms gradually lost autonomy, particularly following the [Rebellion of the Seven States](/wiki/Rebellion_of_the_Seven_States "Rebellion of the Seven States"), with their [fiefs](/wiki/Fief "Fief") progressively fragmented/reduced or completely seized by the central government via [imperial decrees](/wiki/Imperial_decree "Imperial decree") (often under the pretext of criminal [impeachments](/wiki/Impeachment "Impeachment")). From the reign of [Emperor Wu](/wiki/Emperor_Wu_of_Han "Emperor Wu of Han") (r. 141–87 BC) onward, the Han imperial court officially endorsed [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism") in court politics and philosophy, synthesizing it with the [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology") of [Dong Zhongshu](/wiki/Dong_Zhongshu "Dong Zhongshu"). The Han dynasty experienced [periods of economic prosperity](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Han_dynasty "Economy of the Han dynasty") as well as significant growth in the [money economy](/wiki/History_of_Chinese_currency#Imperial_China "History of Chinese currency"), which had first been established during the Zhou dynasty. The *[Wu Zhu](/wiki/Wu_Zhu "Wu Zhu")* [cash coin](/wiki/Cash_(Chinese_coin) "Cash (Chinese coin)") minted by the Han government in 119 BC remained the standard [Chinese coinage](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_coinage "Ancient Chinese coinage") for the following seven centuries until the introduction of the *[Kaiyuan Tongbao](/wiki/Kaiyuan_Tongbao "Kaiyuan Tongbao")* by the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (618–907 AD). To finance its [military campaigns](/wiki/Military_of_the_Han_dynasty#Major_campaigns_and_battles "Military of the Han dynasty") and settlement of newly conquered frontier territories, the Han government nationalized all [salt](/wiki/Salt_in_Chinese_history "Salt in Chinese history") and [iron industries](/wiki/History_of_metallurgy_in_China#Iron "History of metallurgy in China") in 117 BC, creating government [monopolies](/wiki/Monopolies "Monopolies") that were later repealed during the Eastern Han period. There were significant [advances in science and technology](/wiki/Science_and_technology_of_the_Han_dynasty "Science and technology of the Han dynasty") during the Han period, including the invention of [papermaking](/wiki/Papermaking "Papermaking"), [rudders](/wiki/Rudder "Rudder") for steering ships, [negative numbers](/wiki/Negative_number "Negative number") in [mathematics](/wiki/Chinese_mathematics "Chinese mathematics"), [raised-relief maps](/wiki/Raised-relief_map "Raised-relief map"), [hydraulic](/wiki/Hydraulic "Hydraulic")-powered [armillary spheres](/wiki/Armillary_sphere "Armillary sphere") for [astronomy](/wiki/Chinese_astronomy "Chinese astronomy"), and [seismometers](/wiki/Seismometer "Seismometer") that discerned the cardinal direction of distant [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") by use of [inverted pendulums](/wiki/Inverted_pendulum "Inverted pendulum").

The Han dynasty was a significant period of [Chinese expansionism](/wiki/Chinese_expansionism "Chinese expansionism"), during which the [Sinosphere](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere") entered [Central](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), [Northeast](/wiki/Northeast_Asia "Northeast Asia") and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") for the first time in history. The most prominent chapter was the [geostrategic conflicts](/wiki/Han%E2%80%93Xiongnu_Wars "Han–Xiongnu Wars") with the [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu"), a [nomadic empire](/wiki/Nomadic_empire "Nomadic empire") centered in the eastern [Eurasian steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_steppe "Eurasian steppe").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBailey198525–26-8) Under [Modu Chanyu](/wiki/Modu_Chanyu "Modu Chanyu"), the Xiongnu [defeated the Han](/wiki/Battle_of_Baideng "Battle of Baideng") in 200 BC, forcing the Han to [appease](/wiki/Appease "Appease") the Xiongnu with a policy of periodic "[marriage alliance](/wiki/Marriage_alliance "Marriage alliance")" (*[heqin](/wiki/Heqin "Heqin")*) and[tribute](/wiki/Tribute "Tribute") payments, though the Xiongnu continued to raid Han China's northern borders. Han military policy changed from defensive to offensive in 133 BC when Emperor Wu began a series of aggressive military campaigns to strike the Xiongnu. Han forces successfully annexed the [Ordos Plateau](/wiki/Ordos_Plateau "Ordos Plateau"), the [Hexi Corridor](/wiki/Hexi_Corridor "Hexi Corridor") and the [Tocharian](/wiki/Tocharian "Tocharian") [city-states](/wiki/City-state "City-state") of the [Tarim Basin](/wiki/Tarim_Basin "Tarim Basin"), establishing [Northern Silk Road](/wiki/Northern_Silk_Road "Northern Silk Road") [trade routes](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") into the [Western Regions](/wiki/Western_Regions "Western Regions") and [hegemony in Inner Asia](/wiki/Han_dynasty_in_Inner_Asia "Han dynasty in Inner Asia") that culminated in [a regional protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate_of_the_Western_Regions "Protectorate of the Western Regions"). The successes of Han northern and western campaigns forced the defeated Xiongnu tribes to eventually submit as [Han vassals](/wiki/List_of_tributaries_of_China "List of tributaries of China"), though the territories of these Xiongnu subjects were later overrun by the new [Donghu](/wiki/Donghu_people "Donghu people") confederations such as [Wuhuan](/wiki/Wuhuan "Wuhuan") and [Xianbei](/wiki/Xianbei "Xianbei"). Emperor Wu also launched successful [southern conquests](/wiki/Southward_expansion_of_the_Han_dynasty "Southward expansion of the Han dynasty") into what would become present-day [South](/wiki/South_China "South China") and [Southwestern China](/wiki/Southwestern_China "Southwestern China"), permanently annexing [Nanyue in 111 BC](/wiki/Han%E2%80%93Nanyue_War "Han–Nanyue War") and [Dian in 109 BC](/wiki/Han_campaigns_against_Dian "Han campaigns against Dian"), as well as [establishing long-term rule](/wiki/Vietnam_under_Chinese_rule "Vietnam under Chinese rule") over [northern](/wiki/Northern_Vietnam "Northern Vietnam") and [central Vietnam](/wiki/Central_Vietnam "Central Vietnam") in what would be known in [Vietnamese history](/wiki/Vietnamese_history "Vietnamese history") as the [First](/wiki/First_Era_of_Northern_Domination "First Era of Northern Domination") and [Second Era of Northern Domination](/wiki/Second_Era_of_Northern_Domination "Second Era of Northern Domination"). Han territories in the [northeast](/wiki/Northeast_China "Northeast China") was also further expanded into the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula"), where Han forces [conquered Gojoseon](/wiki/Han_conquest_of_Gojoseon "Han conquest of Gojoseon") and established the [four commanderies](/wiki/Four_Commanderies_of_Han "Four Commanderies of Han") of [Xuantu](/wiki/Xuantu "Xuantu"), [Lelang](/wiki/Lelang "Lelang"), [Lintun](/wiki/Lintun "Lintun") and [Zhenfan](/wiki/Zhenfan "Zhenfan") in 108 BC. The Han court also made early contacts with [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period") [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") (then known as [Wa](/wiki/Wa_(name_of_Japan) "Wa (name of Japan)")), particular the [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu") state of [Nakoku](/wiki/Nakoku "Nakoku"), as evidenced by the [King of Na gold seal](/wiki/King_of_Na_gold_seal "King of Na gold seal").

The Han dynasty began declining after a massive [Qiang](/wiki/Qiang_(historical_people) "Qiang (historical people)") rebellion in 107 AD that lasted decades, although its decay already started after 92 AD when palace [eunuchs](/wiki/Eunuchs "Eunuchs") increasingly involved themselves in court politics, engaging in violent power struggles between various [consort clans](/wiki/Consort_clan "Consort clan") of the empresses and [empresses dowagers](/wiki/Empresses_dowager "Empresses dowager"). Imperial authority was also seriously challenged by large grassroots [Taoist](/wiki/Taoist "Taoist") religious societies which instigated the [Yellow Turban Rebellion](/wiki/Yellow_Turban_Rebellion "Yellow Turban Rebellion") and the [Five Pecks of Rice Rebellion](/wiki/Five_Pecks_of_Rice_Rebellion "Five Pecks of Rice Rebellion"). Following the death of [Emperor Ling](/wiki/Emperor_Ling_of_Han "Emperor Ling of Han") (r. 168–189 AD), the palace eunuchs [were massacred](/wiki/History_of_the_Han_dynasty#Downfall_of_the_eunuchs "History of the Han dynasty") by military officers under Grand Marshall [He Jin](/wiki/He_Jin "He Jin"), [Empress He](/wiki/Empress_He_(Han_dynasty) "Empress He (Han dynasty)")'s half-brother and the chief regent of the underaged [Emperor Shao](/wiki/Emperor_Shao_of_Han "Emperor Shao of Han"), after the [Ten Attendants](/wiki/Ten_Attendants "Ten Attendants") assassinated He. The death of both the incumbent supreme commander of the Han military and the rival eunuch clan created a [power vacuum](/wiki/Power_vacuum "Power vacuum") that allowed members of the aristocracy and regional military governors to become [warlords](/wiki/Warlord "Warlord") who [divide up the empire](/wiki/End_of_the_Han_dynasty "End of the Han dynasty") for their personal ambitions. The Han dynasty officially came to an end in 220 AD when [Cao Pi](/wiki/Cao_Pi "Cao Pi"), the king of [Wei](/wiki/Cao_Wei "Cao Wei"), usurped the throne by coercing [Emperor Xian](/wiki/Emperor_Xian "Emperor Xian") (who had been a [puppet ruler](/wiki/Puppet_ruler "Puppet ruler") since childhood) to [abdicate](/wiki/Abdicate "Abdicate") to him, although the title "Han dynasty" would continue nominally as a self-proclaimed [rump state](/wiki/Rump_state "Rump state") founded by Emperor Xian's loyalist distant uncle [Liu Bei](/wiki/Liu_Bei "Liu Bei") called [Shu Han](/wiki/Shu_Han "Shu Han") (221–263 AD), thus starting the [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms") period.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

According to the *[Shiji](/wiki/Shiji "Shiji")*, after the collapse of the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") the hegemon [Xiang Yu](/wiki/Xiang_Yu "Xiang Yu") appointed [Liu Bang](/wiki/Liu_Bang "Liu Bang") as prince of the small fief of [Hanzhong](/wiki/Hanzhong "Hanzhong"), named after its location on the [Han River](/wiki/Han_River_(Shaanxi_and_Hubei) "Han River (Shaanxi and Hubei)") (in modern southwest [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi")). Following Liu Bang's victory in the [Chu–Han Contention](/wiki/Chu%E2%80%93Han_Contention "Chu–Han Contention"), the resulting Han dynasty was named after the Hanzhong fief.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986116-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Han dynasty](/wiki/History_of_the_Han_dynasty "History of the Han dynasty")

Further information: [Timeline of the Han dynasty](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_Han_dynasty "Timeline of the Han dynasty")

### Western Han (202 BC – 9 AD)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Western Han (202 BC – 9 AD)")]

See also: [Han–Xiongnu Wars](/wiki/Han%E2%80%93Xiongnu_Wars "Han–Xiongnu Wars"), [Han dynasty in Inner Asia](/wiki/Han_dynasty_in_Inner_Asia "Han dynasty in Inner Asia"), and [Southward expansion](/wiki/Southward_expansion_of_the_Han_dynasty "Southward expansion of the Han dynasty")

Further information: [Loulan Kingdom](/wiki/Loulan_Kingdom "Loulan Kingdom"), [Shule Kingdom](/wiki/Shule_Kingdom "Shule Kingdom"), [Kingdom of Khotan](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Khotan "Kingdom of Khotan"), [Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka"), and [Tocharians](/wiki/Tocharians "Tocharians")

China's first [imperial dynasty](/wiki/Dynasties_in_Chinese_history "Dynasties in Chinese history") was the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BC). The Qin united the Chinese [Warring States](/wiki/Warring_States "Warring States") by conquest, but their regime became unstable after the death of the first emperor [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang"). Within four years, the dynasty's authority had collapsed in a rebellion.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199960–61-10) Two former rebel leaders, [Xiang Yu](/wiki/Xiang_Yu "Xiang Yu") (d. 202 BC) of [Chu](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Chu "Kingdom of Chu") and [Liu Bang](/wiki/Liu_Bang "Liu Bang") (d. 195 BC) of [Han](/wiki/Hanzhong "Hanzhong"), engaged [in a war](/wiki/Chu%E2%80%93Han_contention "Chu–Han contention") to determine who would have hegemony over China, which had fissured into [Eighteen Kingdoms](/wiki/Eighteen_Kingdoms "Eighteen Kingdoms"), each claiming allegiance to either Xiang Yu or Liu Bang.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986116–122-11) Although Xiang Yu proved to be an effective commander, Liu Bang defeated him at the [Battle of Gaixia](/wiki/Battle_of_Gaixia "Battle of Gaixia") (202 BC) in modern-day [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui"). Liu Bang assumed [the title of Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_China "Emperor of China") at the urging of his followers and is known posthumously as [Emperor Gaozu](/wiki/Emperor_Gaozu_of_Han "Emperor Gaozu of Han") (r. 202–195 BC).[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis200144–46-12) [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an") (modern Xi'an) was chosen as the new capital of the reunified empire under Han.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986122-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Han_dynasty_Kingdoms_195_BC.png/500px-Han_dynasty_Kingdoms_195_BC.png)](/wiki/File:Han_dynasty_Kingdoms_195_BC.png)

Thirteen direct-controlled [commanderies](/wiki/Commandery_(China) "Commandery (China)") including the capital region (yellow) and ten semi-autonomous kingdoms, 195 BC

At the beginning of the **Western Han** ([traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 西漢; [simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 西汉; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Xīhàn*), also known as the **Former Han** (前漢; 前汉; *Qiánhàn*), thirteen centrally controlled [commanderies](/wiki/Commandery_(China) "Commandery (China)")—including the capital region—existed in the western third of the empire, while the eastern two-thirds were divided into ten [semi-autonomous kingdoms](/wiki/Kings_of_the_Han_dynasty "Kings of the Han dynasty").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986122–125-14) To placate his prominent commanders from the war with Chu, Emperor Gaozu [enfeoffed](/wiki/Enfeoffed "Enfeoffed") some of them as kings.

By 196, the Han court had replaced all of these kings with royal [Liu](/wiki/Liu "Liu") family members, with the lone exception of [Changsha](/wiki/Changsha_Kingdom "Changsha Kingdom"). The loyalty of non-relatives to the emperor was questioned,[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986122–125-14) and after several insurrections by Han kings—with the largest being the [Rebellion of the Seven States](/wiki/Rebellion_of_the_Seven_States "Rebellion of the Seven States") in 154—the imperial court began enacting a series of reforms that limited the power of these kingdoms in 145, dividing their former territories into new commanderies under central control.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986139–144-15) Kings were no longer able to appoint their own staff; this duty was assumed by the imperial court.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980106-16)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197276-17) Kings became nominal heads of their [fiefs](/wiki/Fief "Fief") and collected a portion of tax revenues as their personal incomes.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980106-16)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197276-17) The kingdoms were never entirely abolished and existed throughout the remainder of Western and Eastern Han.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980105-18)

To the north of [China proper](/wiki/China_proper "China proper"), the nomadic [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu") chieftain [Modu Chanyu](/wiki/Modu_Chanyu "Modu Chanyu") (r. 209–174 BC) conquered various tribes inhabiting the eastern portion of the [Eurasian Steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_Steppe "Eurasian Steppe"). By the end of his reign, he controlled the [Inner Asian](/wiki/Inner_Asia "Inner Asia") regions of [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria"), [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), and the [Tarim Basin](/wiki/Tarim_Basin "Tarim Basin"), subjugating over twenty states east of [Samarkand](/wiki/Samarkand "Samarkand").[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDi_Cosmo2002175–189,_196–198-19)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorday199780–81-20)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986387–388-21) Emperor Gaozu was troubled about the abundant Han-manufactured iron weapons traded to the Xiongnu along the northern borders, and he established a trade [embargo](/wiki/Embargo "Embargo") against the group.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorday199775–77-22)

In retaliation, the Xiongnu invaded what is now [Shanxi](/wiki/Shanxi "Shanxi"), where they [defeated the Han forces at Baideng](/wiki/Battle_of_Baideng "Battle of Baideng") in 200 BC.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorday199775–77-22)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDi_Cosmo2002190–192-23) After negotiations, the *[heqin](/wiki/Heqin "Heqin")* agreement in 198 BC nominally held the leaders of the Xiongnu and the Han as equal partners in a royal marriage alliance, but the Han were forced to send large amounts of tribute items such as silk clothes, food, and wine to the Xiongnu.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü19679–10-24)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMortonLewis200552-25)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDi_Cosmo2002192–195-26)

Despite the tribute and negotiation between [Laoshang Chanyu](/wiki/Laoshang_Chanyu "Laoshang Chanyu") (r. 174–160 BC) and [Emperor Wen](/wiki/Emperor_Wen_of_Han "Emperor Wen of Han") (r. 180–157 BC) to reopen border markets, many of the [Chanyu](/wiki/Chanyu "Chanyu")'s subordinates chose not to obey the treaty and periodically raided Han territories south of the [Great Wall](/wiki/Great_Wall "Great Wall") for additional goods.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986388–389-27)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorday199777,_82–83-28)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDi_Cosmo2002195–196-29) In a court conference assembled by [Emperor Wu](/wiki/Emperor_Wu_of_Han "Emperor Wu of Han") (r. 141–87 BC) in 135 BC, the [majority consensus](/wiki/Consensus_decision-making "Consensus decision-making") of the ministers was to retain the *heqin* agreement. Emperor Wu accepted this, despite continuing Xiongnu raids.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorday199783–84-30)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986389–390-31)

However, a court conference the following year convinced the majority that a [limited engagement at Mayi](/wiki/Battle_of_Mayi "Battle of Mayi") involving the assassination of the Chanyu would throw the Xiongnu realm into chaos and benefit the Han.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986389–391-32)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDi_Cosmo2002211–214-33) When this plot failed in 133 BC,[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorday199791–92-34) Emperor Wu launched a series of [massive military invasions](/wiki/Han%E2%80%93Xiongnu_War "Han–Xiongnu War") into Xiongnu territory. The assault culminated in 119 BC at the [Battle of Mobei](/wiki/Battle_of_Mobei "Battle of Mobei"), when Han commanders [Huo Qubing](/wiki/Huo_Qubing "Huo Qubing") (d. 117 BC) and [Wei Qing](/wiki/Wei_Qing "Wei Qing") (d. 106 BC) forced the Xiongnu court to flee north of the [Gobi Desert](/wiki/Gobi_Desert "Gobi Desert"), and Han forces reached as far north as [Lake Baikal](/wiki/Lake_Baikal "Lake Baikal").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986390-35)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDi_Cosmo2002237–240-36)

After Wu's reign, Han forces continued to fight the Xiongnu. The Xiongnu leader [Huhanye](/wiki/Huhanye "Huhanye") (r. 58–31 BC) finally submitted to the Han as a tributary vassal in 51 BC. Huhanye's rival claimant to the throne, [Zhizhi Chanyu](/wiki/Zhizhi_Chanyu "Zhizhi Chanyu") (r. 56–36 BC), was killed by Han forces under [Chen Tang](/wiki/Chen_Tang_(general) "Chen Tang (general)") and [Gan Yanshou](/wiki/Gan_Yanshou "Gan Yanshou") at the [Battle of Zhizhi](/wiki/Battle_of_Zhizhi "Battle of Zhizhi"), in modern [Taraz](/wiki/Taraz "Taraz"), Kazakhstan.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986196–197,_211–213-37)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986395–398-38)

In 121 BC, Han forces expelled the Xiongnu from a vast territory spanning the [Hexi Corridor](/wiki/Hexi_Corridor "Hexi Corridor") to [Lop Nur](/wiki/Lop_Nur "Lop Nur"). They repelled a joint Xiongnu-[Qiang](/wiki/Qiang_(historical_people) "Qiang (historical people)") invasion of this northwestern territory in 111 BC. In that same year, the Han court established four new frontier commanderies in this region to consolidate their control: [Jiuquan](/wiki/Jiuquan "Jiuquan"), [Zhangyi](/wiki/Zhangyi "Zhangyi"), [Dunhuang](/wiki/Dunhuang "Dunhuang"), and [Wuwei](/wiki/Wuwei,_Gansu "Wuwei, Gansu").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChang20075–8-39)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDi_Cosmo2002241–242-40)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986391-41) The majority of people on the frontier were soldiers.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChang200734–35-42) On occasion, the court forcibly moved peasant farmers to new frontier settlements, along with government-owned slaves and convicts who performed hard labour.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChang20076,_15–16,_44–45-43) The court also encouraged commoners, such as farmers, merchants, landowners, and hired labourers, to voluntarily migrate to the frontier.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChang200715–16,_33–35,_42–43-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Huo_Qubing_horse_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Huo_Qubing_horse_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huo_Qubing_horse_(cropped).jpg)

Statue of a horse trampling a [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu") warrior, at the mausoleum of Western Han general [Huo Qubing](/wiki/Huo_Qubing "Huo Qubing") (d. 117 BC), who fought in the [Han–Xiongnu War](/wiki/Han%E2%80%93Xiongnu_War "Han–Xiongnu War"). This is the first known monumental stone statue in China.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQingbo2023-45)

Even before the Han's expansion into Central Asia, diplomat [Zhang Qian](/wiki/Zhang_Qian "Zhang Qian")'s travels from 139 to 125 BC had established Chinese contacts with many surrounding civilizations. Zhang encountered [Dayuan](/wiki/Dayuan "Dayuan") ([Fergana](/wiki/Fergana "Fergana")), [Kangju](/wiki/Kangju "Kangju") ([Sogdiana](/wiki/Sogdiana "Sogdiana")), and [Daxia](/wiki/Daxia "Daxia") ([Bactria](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria"), formerly the [Greco-Bactrian Kingdom](/wiki/Greco-Bactrian_Kingdom "Greco-Bactrian Kingdom")); he also gathered information on Shendu (the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") valley) and Anxi (the [Parthian Empire](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire")). All of these countries eventually received Han embassies.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDi_Cosmo2002247–249-46)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMortonLewis200554–55-47)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986407-48)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199969-49)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorday1997104–117-50) These connections marked the beginning of the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road") trade network that extended to the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), bringing goods like Chinese silk and Roman glasswares between the two.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAn200283-51)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199970-52)

From c. 115 BC until c. 60 BC, Han forces fought the Xiongnu over control of the oasis city-states in the Tarim Basin. The Han were eventually victorious and established the [Protectorate of the Western Regions](/wiki/Protectorate_of_the_Western_Regions "Protectorate of the Western Regions") in 60 BC, which dealt with the region's defence and foreign affairs.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDi_Cosmo2002250–251-53)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986390–391,_409–411-54)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChang2007174-55)[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986198-56) The Han also [expanded southward](/wiki/Southward_expansion_of_the_Han_dynasty "Southward expansion of the Han dynasty"). The [naval conquest of Nanyue](/wiki/Han%E2%80%93Nanyue_War "Han–Nanyue War") in 111 BC expanded the Han realm into what are now modern [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi"), and northern Vietnam. [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") was brought into the Han realm with the [conquest](/wiki/Han_campaigns_against_Dian "Han campaigns against Dian") of the [Dian Kingdom](/wiki/Dian_Kingdom "Dian Kingdom") in 109 BC, followed by parts of the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula") with the [Han conquest of Gojoseon](/wiki/Han_conquest_of_Gojoseon "Han conquest of Gojoseon") and establishment of the [Xuantu](/wiki/Xuantu "Xuantu") and [Lelang commanderies](/wiki/Lelang_Commandery "Lelang Commandery") in 108 BC.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199983-57)[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986448–453-58) The first nationwide census in Chinese history was taken in 2 AD; the Han's total population was registered as comprising 57,671,400 individuals across 12,366,470 households.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986595–596-59)

To pay for his military campaigns and colonial expansion, Emperor Wu [nationalised](/wiki/Nationalised "Nationalised") several private industries. He created central government [monopolies](/wiki/Monopolies "Monopolies") administered largely by [former merchants](/wiki/Four_occupations "Four occupations"). These monopolies included salt, iron, and liquor production, as well as [bronze coinage](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_coinage "Ancient Chinese coinage"). The liquor monopoly lasted only from 98 to 81 BC, and the salt and iron monopolies were eventually abolished in the early Eastern Han. The issuing of coinage remained a central government monopoly throughout the rest of the Han dynasty.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWagner20011–17-60)[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986160–161-61)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986581–588-62)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199975-63)[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMortonLewis200557-64)[[b]](#cite_note-65)

The government monopolies were eventually repealed when a political faction known as the Reformists gained greater influence in the court. The Reformists opposed the Modernist faction that had dominated court politics in Emperor Wu's reign and during the subsequent [regency](/wiki/Regency "Regency") of [Huo Guang](/wiki/Huo_Guang "Huo Guang") (d. 68 BC). The Modernists argued for an aggressive and expansionary foreign policy supported by revenues from heavy government intervention in the private economy. The Reformists, however, overturned these policies, favouring a cautious, non-expansionary approach to foreign policy, frugal [budget](/wiki/Government_budget "Government budget") reform, and lower tax-rates imposed on private entrepreneurs.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986162,_185–206-66)[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaludan199841-67)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWagner200116–19-68)

### Wang Mang's reign and civil war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Wang Mang's reign and civil war")]

Main articles: [Wang Mang](/wiki/Wang_Mang "Wang Mang") and [Xin dynasty](/wiki/Xin_dynasty "Xin dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Han_Dynasty_Granary_west_of_Dunhuang.jpg/500px-Han_Dynasty_Granary_west_of_Dunhuang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Han_Dynasty_Granary_west_of_Dunhuang.jpg)

These [rammed earth](/wiki/Rammed_earth "Rammed earth") ruins of a [granary](/wiki/Granary "Granary") in Hecang Fortress (河仓城; *Hécāng chéng*), located approximately 11 km (7 mi) northeast of the Western Han-era [Yumen Pass](/wiki/Yumen_Pass "Yumen Pass"), were built during the Western Han (202 BC – 9 AD) and was significantly rebuilt during the [Western Jin](/wiki/Western_Jin "Western Jin") (280–316 AD).[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWangLiZhang2010351–352-69)

[Wang Zhengjun](/wiki/Wang_Zhengjun "Wang Zhengjun") (71 BC – 13 AD) was first empress, then [empress dowager](/wiki/Empress_dowager "Empress dowager"), and finally [grand empress dowager](/wiki/Grand_empress_dowager "Grand empress dowager") during the reigns of the Emperors [Yuan](/wiki/Emperor_Yuan_of_Han "Emperor Yuan of Han") (r. 49–33 BC), [Cheng](/wiki/Emperor_Cheng_of_Han "Emperor Cheng of Han") (r. 33–7 BC), and [Ai](/wiki/Emperor_Ai_of_Han "Emperor Ai of Han") (r. 7–1 BC), respectively. During this time, a succession of her male relatives held the title of regent.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986225–226-70)[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuang198846–48-71) Following the death of Ai, Wang Zhengjun's nephew [Wang Mang](/wiki/Wang_Mang "Wang Mang") (45 BC – 23 AD) was appointed regent as Marshall of State on 16 August under [Emperor Ping](/wiki/Emperor_Ping "Emperor Ping") (r. 1 BC – 6 AD).[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986227–230-72)

When Ping died on 3 February 6 AD, [Ruzi Ying](/wiki/Ruzi_Ying "Ruzi Ying") (d. 25 AD) was chosen as the heir and Wang Mang was appointed to serve as acting emperor for the child.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986227–230-72) Wang promised to relinquish his control to Liu Ying once he came of age.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986227–230-72) Despite this promise, and against protest and revolts from the nobility, Wang Mang claimed on 10 January that the divine [Mandate of Heaven](/wiki/Mandate_of_Heaven "Mandate of Heaven") called for the end of the Han dynasty and the beginning of his own: the [Xin dynasty](/wiki/Xin_dynasty "Xin dynasty") (9–23 AD).[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200223–24-73)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986230–231-74)[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199966-75)

Wang Mang initiated a series of major reforms that were ultimately unsuccessful. These reforms included outlawing slavery, [nationalizing](/wiki/Nationalizing "Nationalizing") and [equally distributing](/wiki/Well-field_system "Well-field system") land between households, and introducing new currencies, a change which debased the value of coinage.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000134-76)[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986232–234-77)[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMortonLewis200558-78)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis200723-79) Although these reforms provoked considerable opposition, Wang's regime met its ultimate downfall with the massive floods of c. 3 AD and 11 AD. Gradual silt build-up in the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") had raised its water level and overwhelmed the [flood control works](/wiki/Flood_control "Flood control"). The Yellow River split into two new branches: one emptying to the north and the other to the south of the [Shandong Peninsula](/wiki/Shandong_Peninsula "Shandong Peninsula"), though Han engineers managed to dam the southern branch by 70 AD.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000135-80)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007196-81)[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986241–244-82)

The flood dislodged thousands of peasant farmers, many of whom joined roving bandit and rebel groups such as the [Red Eyebrows](/wiki/Red_Eyebrows "Red Eyebrows") to survive.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000135-80)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007196-81)[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986241–244-82) Wang Mang's armies were incapable of quelling these enlarged rebel groups. Eventually, an insurgent mob forced their way into the [Weiyang Palace](/wiki/Weiyang_Palace "Weiyang Palace") and killed Wang Mang.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007568-83)[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986248-84)

The [Gengshi Emperor](/wiki/Gengshi_Emperor "Gengshi Emperor") (r. 23–25 AD), a descendant of [Emperor Jing](/wiki/Emperor_Jing_of_Han "Emperor Jing of Han") (r. 157–141 BC), attempted to restore the Han dynasty and occupied Chang'an as his capital. However, he was overwhelmed by the Red Eyebrow rebels who deposed, assassinated, and replaced him with the puppet monarch [Liu Penzi](/wiki/Liu_Penzi "Liu Penzi").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007197,_560-85)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986249–250-86) Gengshi's distant cousin Liu Xiu, known posthumously as [Emperor Guangwu](/wiki/Emperor_Guangwu "Emperor Guangwu") (r. 25–57 AD), after distinguishing himself at the [Battle of Kunyang](/wiki/Battle_of_Kunyang "Battle of Kunyang") in 23 AD, was urged to succeed Gengshi as emperor.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007558–560-87)[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986251–254-88)

Under Guangwu's rule, the Han Empire was restored. Guangwu made [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang") his capital in 25 AD, and by 27 his officers [Deng Yu](/wiki/Deng_Yu "Deng Yu") and [Feng Yi](/wiki/Feng_Yi "Feng Yi") had forced the Red Eyebrows to surrender and executed their leaders for [treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason").[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986251–254-88)[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007196–198,_560-89) From 26 until 36 AD, Emperor Guangwu had to wage war against other regional warlords who claimed the title of emperor; when these warlords were defeated, China reunified under the Han.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny200754–55,_269–270,_600–601-90)[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986254–255-91)

The period between the foundation of the Han dynasty and Wang Mang's reign is known as the Western Han ([西漢](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%A5%BF%E6%BC%A2 "wikt:西漢"); [西汉](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%A5%BF%E6%B1%89 "wikt:西汉"); *Xīhàn*) or Former Han ([前漢](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%89%8D%E6%BC%A2 "wikt:前漢"); [前汉](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%89%8D%E6%B1%89 "wikt:前汉"); *Qiánhàn*) (206 BC – 9 AD). During this period the capital was at [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an") (modern [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an")). From the reign of Guangwu the capital was moved eastward to Luoyang. The era from his reign until [the fall of Han](/wiki/End_of_the_Han_dynasty "End of the Han dynasty") is known as the Eastern Han or Later Han (25–220 AD).[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200224–25-92)

### Eastern Han (25–220 AD)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Eastern Han (25–220 AD)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Bronze_seal_of_a_Xiongnu_chief_%28seal%2C_reverse_image%2C_transcription%29.jpg/500px-Bronze_seal_of_a_Xiongnu_chief_%28seal%2C_reverse_image%2C_transcription%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bronze_seal_of_a_Xiongnu_chief_(seal,_reverse_image,_transcription).jpg)

[Bronze seal](/wiki/Seal_(East_Asia) "Seal (East Asia)") of a Xiongnu chieftain with impression and transcription, conferred by the Eastern Han government and inscribed with the following text: 漢匈奴，歸義親，漢長 ("The Chief of the Han Xiongnu, who have returned to righteousness and embraced the Han")[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPsarras201519-93)

The **Eastern Han** ([traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 東漢; [simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 东汉; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Dōnghàn*), also known as the **Later Han** (後漢; 后汉; *Hòuhàn*), formally began on 5 August AD 25, when Liu Xiu became [Emperor Guangwu of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Guangwu_of_Han "Emperor Guangwu of Han").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnechtges2010116-94) During the widespread rebellion against [Wang Mang](/wiki/Wang_Mang "Wang Mang"), the state of [Goguryeo](/wiki/Goguryeo "Goguryeo") was free to raid Han's [Korean commanderies](/wiki/Four_Commanderies_of_Han "Four Commanderies of Han"); Han did not reaffirm its control over the region until AD 30.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986450-95)

The [Trưng Sisters](/wiki/Tr%C6%B0ng_Sisters "Trưng Sisters") of [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") rebelled against Han in AD 40. Their rebellion was crushed by Han general [Ma Yuan](/wiki/Ma_Yuan_(Han_dynasty) "Ma Yuan (Han dynasty)") (d. AD 49) in a campaign from AD 42 to 43.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007562,_660-96)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986454-97) Wang Mang renewed hostilities against the [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu"), who were estranged from Han until their leader Bi (比), a rival claimant to the throne against his cousin Punu (蒲奴), submitted to Han as a tributary vassal in AD 50. This created two rival Xiongnu states: the Southern Xiongnu led by Bi, an ally of Han, and the Northern Xiongnu led by Punu, an enemy of Han.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986237–238-98)[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986399–400-99)

During the turbulent reign of Wang Mang, China lost control over the Tarim Basin, which was conquered by the Northern Xiongnu in AD 63 and used as a base to invade the Hexi Corridor in [Gansu](/wiki/Gansu "Gansu").[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986413–414-100) [Dou Gu](/wiki/Dou_Gu "Dou Gu") (d. 88 AD) defeated the Northern Xiongnu at the [Battle of Yiwulu](/wiki/Battle_of_Yiwulu "Battle of Yiwulu") in AD 73, evicting them from [Turpan](/wiki/Turpan "Turpan") and chasing them as far as [Lake Barkol](/wiki/Barkol_Kazakh_Autonomous_County "Barkol Kazakh Autonomous County") before establishing a garrison at Hami.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986414–415-101) After the new Protector General of the Western Regions [Chen Mu](/wiki/Chen_Mu "Chen Mu") (d. AD 75) was killed by allies of the Xiongnu in [Karasahr](/wiki/Karasahr "Karasahr") and [Kucha](/wiki/Kucha "Kucha"), the garrison at Hami was withdrawn.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986414–415-101)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny200773-102)

At the [Battle of Ikh Bayan](/wiki/Battle_of_Ikh_Bayan "Battle of Ikh Bayan") in AD 89, [Dou Xian](/wiki/Dou_Xian "Dou Xian") (d. AD 92) defeated the [Northern Xiongnu *chanyu*](/wiki/Northern_Chanyu_(1st_century) "Northern Chanyu (1st century)") who then retreated into the [Altai Mountains](/wiki/Altai_Mountains "Altai Mountains").[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986414–415-101)[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007171-103) After the Northern Xiongnu fled into the [Ili River](/wiki/Ili_River "Ili River") valley in AD 91, the nomadic [Xianbei](/wiki/Xianbei "Xianbei") occupied the area from the borders of the [Buyeo Kingdom](/wiki/Buyeo_Kingdom "Buyeo Kingdom") in Manchuria to the Ili River of the [Wusun](/wiki/Wusun "Wusun") people.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986405,_443–444-104) The Xianbei reached their apogee under [Tanshihuai](/wiki/Tanshihuai "Tanshihuai") (d. AD 181), who consistently defeated Chinese armies. However, Tanshihuai's confederation disintegrated after his death.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986444–446-105)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Lead_Ingot_with_Characters%2C_Gansu_Provincial_Museum.jpg/250px-Lead_Ingot_with_Characters%2C_Gansu_Provincial_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lead_Ingot_with_Characters,_Gansu_Provincial_Museum.jpg)

Eastern Han inscriptions on a lead ingot, using the [Greek alphabet](/wiki/History_of_the_Greek_alphabet "History of the Greek alphabet") in the style of the [Kushans](/wiki/Kushans "Kushans"), excavated in Shaanxi, 1st–2nd centuries AD – [Gansu Provincial Museum](/wiki/Gansu_Provincial_Museum "Gansu Provincial Museum")[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECribb197876–78-106)

[Ban Chao](/wiki/Ban_Chao "Ban Chao") (d. AD 102) enlisted the aid of the [Kushan Empire](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire"), which controlled territory across South and Central Asia, to subdue [Kashgar](/wiki/Kashgar "Kashgar") and its ally Sogdiana.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorday1997393-107)[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20075–6-108) When a request by Kushan ruler [Vima Kadphises](/wiki/Vima_Kadphises "Vima Kadphises") (r. c. 90 – c. 100 AD) for a marriage alliance with the Han was rejected in AD 90, he sent his forces to [Wakhan](/wiki/Wakhan "Wakhan") (modern-day Afghanistan) to attack Ban Chao. The conflict ended with the Kushans withdrawing because of lack of supplies.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorday1997393-107)[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20075–6-108) In AD 91, the office of Protector General of the Western Regions was reinstated when it was bestowed on Ban Chao.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986415–416-109)

Foreign travellers to the Eastern Han empire included [Buddhist monks](/wiki/Buddhist_monks "Buddhist monks") who [translated works into Chinese](/wiki/Silk_Road_transmission_of_Buddhism "Silk Road transmission of Buddhism"), such as [An Shigao](/wiki/An_Shigao "An Shigao") from Parthia, and [Lokaksema](/wiki/Lokaksema_(Buddhist_monk) "Lokaksema (Buddhist monk)") from Kushan-era [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara").[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkira1998248,_251-110)[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhang200275-111) In addition to tributary relations with the Kushans, the Han empire received gifts from sovereigns in the [Parthian Empire](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire"), as well as from kings in modern [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") and [Japan](/wiki/Wa_(name_of_Japan) "Wa (name of Japan)"). He also initiated an unsuccessful mission to [Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") in AD 97 with [Gan Ying](/wiki/Gan_Ying "Gan Ying") as emissary.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007239–240,_497,_590-112)[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986450–451,_460–461-113)

A [Roman embassy](/wiki/Sino-Roman_relations "Sino-Roman relations") of Emperor [Marcus Aurelius](/wiki/Marcus_Aurelius "Marcus Aurelius") (r. 161–180 AD) is recorded in the *[Weilüe](/wiki/Weil%C3%BCe "Weilüe")* and *[Book of Later Han](/wiki/Book_of_Later_Han "Book of Later Han")* to have reached the court of [Emperor Huan of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Huan_of_Han "Emperor Huan of Han") (r. 146–168 AD) in AD 166,[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChavannes1907185-114) yet [Rafe de Crespigny](/wiki/Rafe_de_Crespigny "Rafe de Crespigny") asserts that this was most likely a group of [Roman merchants](/wiki/Roman_commerce "Roman commerce").[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007600-115)[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYü1986460–461-116) In addition to [Roman glasswares](/wiki/Roman_glass "Roman glass") and [coins](/wiki/Roman_currency "Roman currency") found in China,[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAn200283–84-117)[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBall2016153-118) Roman medallions from the reign of [Antoninus Pius](/wiki/Antoninus_Pius "Antoninus Pius") and his adopted son Marcus Aurelius have been found at [Óc Eo](/wiki/%C3%93c_Eo "Óc Eo") in Vietnam.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBall2016153-118)[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoung200183–84-119) This was near the commandery of [Rinan](/wiki/Rinan "Rinan") where Chinese sources claim the Romans first landed, as well as embassies from [Tianzhu](/wiki/Tianzhu_(India) "Tianzhu (India)") in northern India in 159 and 161.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYule191552-120) Óc Eo is also thought to be the port city "[Cattigara](/wiki/Cattigara "Cattigara")" described by [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") in his *[Geography](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)")* (c. 150 AD) as lying east of the [Golden Chersonese](/wiki/Golden_Chersonese "Golden Chersonese") ([Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula")) along the *[Magnus Sinus](/wiki/Magnus_Sinus "Magnus Sinus")* (i.e. the [Gulf of Thailand](/wiki/Gulf_of_Thailand "Gulf of Thailand") and [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea")), where a Greek sailor had visited.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoung200129-121)[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMawer201338-122)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESuárez199992-123)[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Reilly200797-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Dahuting_mural%2C_Eastern_Han_Dynasty.jpg/250px-Dahuting_mural%2C_Eastern_Han_Dynasty.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dahuting_mural,_Eastern_Han_Dynasty.jpg)

A mural showing women dressed in traditional *[hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")* from the late Eastern Han-era Dahuting Tomb in [Zhengzhou](/wiki/Zhengzhou "Zhengzhou"), Henan

[Emperor Zhang](/wiki/Emperor_Zhang "Emperor Zhang")'s (r. 75–88 AD) reign came to be viewed by later Eastern Han scholars as the high point of the dynastic house.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007497,_500,_592-125) Subsequent reigns were increasingly marked by [eunuch](/wiki/Eunuch "Eunuch") intervention in court politics and their involvement in the violent power struggles of the imperial [consort clans](/wiki/Consort_clan "Consort clan").[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200225-126)[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000136-127) In 92 AD, with the aid of the eunuch [Zheng Zhong](/wiki/Zheng_Zhong "Zheng Zhong") (d. 107 AD), [Emperor He](/wiki/Emperor_He_of_Han "Emperor He of Han") (r. 88–105 AD) had [Empress Dowager Dou](/wiki/Empress_Dou_(Zhang) "Empress Dou (Zhang)") (d. 97 AD) put under [house arrest](/wiki/House_arrest "House arrest") and her clan stripped of power. This was in revenge for Dou's purging of the clan of his natural mother—[Consort Liang](/wiki/Consort_Liang "Consort Liang")—and then concealing her identity from him.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986280–283-128)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007499,_588–589-129) After Emperor He's death, his wife [Empress Deng Sui](/wiki/Empress_Deng_Sui "Empress Deng Sui") (d. 121 AD) managed state affairs as the regent empress dowager during a turbulent financial crisis and widespread Qiang rebellion that lasted from 107 to 118 AD.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986283–284-130)[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007123–127-131)

When Empress Dowager Deng died, [Emperor An](/wiki/Emperor_An_of_Han "Emperor An of Han") (r. 106–125 AD) was convinced by the accusations of the eunuchs Li Run (李閏) and Jiang Jing (江京) that Deng and her family had planned to depose him. An dismissed Deng's clan members from office, exiled them, and forced many to commit suicide.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986284-132)[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007128,_580-133) After An's death, his wife, [Empress Dowager Yan](/wiki/Empress_Yan_Ji "Empress Yan Ji") (d. 126 AD) placed the child [Marquess of Beixiang](/wiki/Marquess_of_Beixiang "Marquess of Beixiang") on the throne in an attempt to retain power within her family. However, palace eunuch [Sun Cheng](/wiki/Sun_Cheng "Sun Cheng") (d. 132 AD) masterminded a successful overthrow of her regime to enthrone [Emperor Shun of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Shun_of_Han "Emperor Shun of Han") (r. 125–144 AD). Yan was placed under house arrest, her relatives were either killed or exiled, and her eunuch allies were slaughtered.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986284–285-134)[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007473–474,_582–583-135) The regent [Liang Ji](/wiki/Liang_Ji "Liang Ji") (d. 159 AD), brother of [Empress Liang Na](/wiki/Empress_Liang_Na "Empress Liang Na") (d. 150 AD), had the brother-in-law of [Consort Deng Mengnü](/wiki/Empress_Deng_Mengn%C3%BC "Empress Deng Mengnü") (d. 165 AD) killed after Deng Mengnü resisted Liang Ji's attempts to control her. Afterward, Emperor Huan employed eunuchs to depose Liang Ji, who was then forced to commit suicide.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986285–286-136)[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007597–598-137)

Students from the [imperial university](/wiki/Taixue "Taixue") organized a widespread [student protest](/wiki/Student_protest "Student protest") against the eunuchs of Emperor Huan's court.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000141-138) Huan further alienated the bureaucracy when he initiated grandiose construction projects and hosted thousands of concubines in his harem at a time of economic crisis.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007597,_599,_601–602-139)[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000141–142-140) Palace eunuchs imprisoned the official Li Ying (李膺) and his associates from the Imperial University on a dubious charge of treason. In 167 AD, the Grand Commandant [Dou Wu](/wiki/Dou_Wu "Dou Wu") (d. 168 AD) convinced his son-in-law, Emperor Huan, to release them.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007602-141) However, the emperor permanently barred Li Ying and his associates from serving in office, marking the beginning of the [Partisan Prohibitions](/wiki/Disasters_of_Partisan_Prohibitions "Disasters of Partisan Prohibitions").[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007602-141)

Following Huan's death, Dou Wu and the Grand Tutor [Chen Fan](/wiki/Chen_Fan "Chen Fan") (d. 168 AD) attempted a coup against the eunuchs [Hou Lan](/wiki/Hou_Lan "Hou Lan") (d. 172 AD), [Cao Jie](/wiki/Cao_Jie_(eunuch) "Cao Jie (eunuch)") (d. 181 AD), and Wang Fu (王甫). When the plot was uncovered, the eunuchs arrested [Empress Dowager Dou](/wiki/Empress_Dou_Miao "Empress Dou Miao") (d. 172 AD) and Chen Fan. General Zhang Huan (張奐) favoured the eunuchs. He and his troops confronted Dou Wu and his retainers at the palace gate where each side shouted accusations of treason against the other. When the retainers gradually deserted Dou Wu, he was forced to commit suicide.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986319–322-142)

Under [Emperor Ling](/wiki/Emperor_Ling_of_Han "Emperor Ling of Han") (r. 168–189 AD) the eunuchs had the partisan prohibitions renewed and expanded, while also auctioning off top government offices.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007511-143)[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986323-144) Many affairs of state were entrusted to the eunuchs [Zhao Zhong](/wiki/Zhao_Zhong "Zhao Zhong") (d. 189 AD) and [Zhang Rang](/wiki/Zhang_Rang "Zhang Rang") (d. 189 AD) while Emperor Ling spent much of his time [roleplaying](/wiki/Sexual_roleplay "Sexual roleplay") with concubines and participating in military parades.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007513–514-145)

### End of the Han dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: End of the Han dynasty")]

Main article: [End of the Han dynasty](/wiki/End_of_the_Han_dynasty "End of the Han dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Jian%27an_Commanderies.svg/500px-Jian%27an_Commanderies.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Jian%27an_Commanderies.svg)

[Provinces and commanderies](/wiki/List_of_provinces_and_commanderies_of_the_Han_dynasty "List of provinces and commanderies of the Han dynasty") in 219 AD, the penultimate year of the Han dynasty

The Partisan Prohibitions were repealed during the [Yellow Turban Rebellion](/wiki/Yellow_Turban_Rebellion "Yellow Turban Rebellion") and [Five Pecks of Rice Rebellion](/wiki/Five_Pecks_of_Rice_Rebellion "Five Pecks of Rice Rebellion") in 184 AD, largely because the court did not want to continue to alienate a significant portion of the [gentry class](/wiki/Four_occupations "Four occupations") who might otherwise join the rebellions.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007511-143) The Yellow Turbans and Five-Pecks-of-Rice adherents belonged to two different hierarchical [Taoist](/wiki/Taoist "Taoist") religious societies led by faith healers [Zhang Jue](/wiki/Zhang_Jue "Zhang Jue") (d. 184 AD) and [Zhang Lu](/wiki/Zhang_Lu_(Three_Kingdoms) "Zhang Lu (Three Kingdoms)") (d. 216 AD), respectively.

Zhang Lu's rebellion, in what is now northern [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan") and southern [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"), was not quelled until 215 AD.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1986628–629-146) Zhang Jue's massive rebellion across eight [provinces](/wiki/Government_of_the_Han_dynasty#Provincial_authorities "Government of the Han dynasty") was annihilated by Han forces within a year; however, the following decades saw much smaller recurrent uprisings.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986339–340-147) Although the Yellow Turbans were defeated, many generals appointed during the crisis never disbanded their assembled militias and used these troops to amass power outside of the collapsing imperial authority.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199984-148)

General-in-chief [He Jin](/wiki/He_Jin "He Jin") (d. 189 AD), half-brother to [Empress He](/wiki/Empress_He_(Han_dynasty) "Empress He (Han dynasty)") (d. 189 AD), plotted with [Yuan Shao](/wiki/Yuan_Shao "Yuan Shao") (d. 202 AD) to overthrow the eunuchs by having several generals march to the outskirts of the capital. There, in a written petition to Empress He, they demanded the eunuchs' execution.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986339–344-149) After a period of hesitation, Empress He consented. When the eunuchs discovered this, however, they had her brother He Miao (何苗) rescind the order.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986344-150) The eunuchs assassinated He Jin on 22 September 189.

Yuan Shao then besieged Luoyang's Northern Palace while his brother [Yuan Shu](/wiki/Yuan_Shu "Yuan Shu") (d. 199 AD) besieged the Southern Palace. On September 25 both palaces were breached and approximately two thousand eunuchs were killed.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986344–345-151)[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMortonLewis200562-152) Zhang Rang had previously fled with [Emperor Shao](/wiki/Liu_Bian "Liu Bian") (r. 189 AD) and his brother Liu Xie—the future [Emperor Xian of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Xian_of_Han "Emperor Xian of Han") (r. 189–220 AD). While being pursued by the Yuan brothers, Zhang committed suicide by jumping into the Yellow River.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986345-153)

General [Dong Zhuo](/wiki/Dong_Zhuo "Dong Zhuo") (d. 192 AD) found the young emperor and his brother wandering in the countryside. He escorted them safely back to the capital and was made [Minister of Works](/wiki/Government_of_the_Han_dynasty#Excellency_of_Works "Government of the Han dynasty"), taking control of Luoyang and forcing Yuan Shao to flee.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986345–346-154) After Dong Zhuo demoted Emperor Shao and promoted his brother Liu Xie as Emperor Xian, Yuan Shao led a coalition of former officials and officers against Dong, who burned Luoyang to the ground and resettled the court at Chang'an in May 191 AD. Dong Zhuo later poisoned Emperor Shao.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986346–349-155)

Dong was killed by his adopted son [Lü Bu](/wiki/L%C3%BC_Bu "Lü Bu") (d. 198 AD) in a plot hatched by [Wang Yun](/wiki/Wang_Yun_(Han_dynasty) "Wang Yun (Han dynasty)") (d. 192 AD).[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007158-156) Emperor Xian fled from Chang'an in 195 AD to the ruins of Luoyang. Xian was persuaded by [Cao Cao](/wiki/Cao_Cao "Cao Cao") (155–220 AD), then Governor of Yan Province in modern western [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong") and eastern [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan"), to move the capital to [Xuchang](/wiki/Xuchang "Xuchang") in 196 AD.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986349–351-157)[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny200736-158)

Yuan Shao challenged Cao Cao for control over the emperor. Yuan's power was greatly diminished after Cao defeated him at the [Battle of Guandu](/wiki/Battle_of_Guandu "Battle of Guandu") in 200 AD. After Yuan died, Cao killed Yuan Shao's son [Yuan Tan](/wiki/Yuan_Tan "Yuan Tan") (173–205 AD), who had fought with his brothers over the family inheritance.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986351–352-159)[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny200736–37-160) His brothers [Yuan Shang](/wiki/Yuan_Shang "Yuan Shang") and [Yuan Xi](/wiki/Yuan_Xi "Yuan Xi") were killed in 207 AD by [Gongsun Kang](/wiki/Gongsun_Kang "Gongsun Kang") (d. 221 AD), who sent their heads to Cao Cao.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986351–352-159)[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny200736–37-160)

After Cao's defeat at the naval [Battle of Red Cliffs](/wiki/Battle_of_Red_Cliffs "Battle of Red Cliffs") in 208 AD, China was divided into three spheres of influence, with Cao Cao dominating the north, [Sun Quan](/wiki/Sun_Quan "Sun Quan") (182–252 AD) dominating the south, and [Liu Bei](/wiki/Liu_Bei "Liu Bei") (161–223 AD) dominating the west.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986352-161)[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny200737-162) Cao Cao died in March 220 AD. By December his son [Cao Pi](/wiki/Cao_Pi "Cao Pi") (187–226 AD) had Emperor Xian relinquish the throne to him and is known posthumously as [Emperor Wen of Wei](/wiki/Emperor_Wen_of_Wei "Emperor Wen of Wei"). This formally ended the Han dynasty and initiated an age of conflict between the [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms"): [Cao Wei](/wiki/Cao_Wei "Cao Wei"), [Eastern Wu](/wiki/Eastern_Wu "Eastern Wu"), and [Shu Han](/wiki/Shu_Han "Shu Han").[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck1986353–357-163)[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch2002206-164)

## Culture and society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Culture and society")]

Main article: [Society and culture of the Han dynasty](/wiki/Society_and_culture_of_the_Han_dynasty "Society and culture of the Han dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Dahuting_tomb_banquet_scene%2C_Eastern_Han_mural.jpg/960px-Dahuting_tomb_banquet_scene%2C_Eastern_Han_mural.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dahuting_tomb_banquet_scene,_Eastern_Han_mural.jpg)

A late Eastern Han mural in a tomb, showing lively scenes of a banquet (*yànyǐn* 宴飲), dance and music (*wǔyuè* 舞樂), acrobatics (*bǎixì* 百戲), and wrestling (*xiāngbū* 相撲), from the [Dahuting](/wiki/Dahuting "Dahuting") tomb in [Zhengzhou](/wiki/Zhengzhou "Zhengzhou"), Henan

### Social class

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Social class")]

See also: [Chinese nobility](/wiki/Chinese_nobility "Chinese nobility"), [Marquis Baocheng](/wiki/Duke_Yansheng#Qin_dynasty_.28221.E2.80.93206_BCE.29_and_the_Western_and_Eastern_Han_dynasties_.28206_BCE_.E2.80.93_220_CE.29 "Duke Yansheng"), and [Four occupations](/wiki/Four_occupations "Four occupations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Eastern_Han_Luoyang_Mural_of_Liubo_players.jpg/250px-Eastern_Han_Luoyang_Mural_of_Liubo_players.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eastern_Han_Luoyang_Mural_of_Liubo_players.jpg)

A mural from an Eastern Han tomb at Zhucun (朱村), Luoyang; the two figures in the foreground are playing *[liubo](/wiki/Liubo "Liubo")*, with the playing mat between them, and the *liubo* game board to the side of the mat.

In the hierarchical social order, the emperor was at the apex of Han society and government. However, the emperor was often a minor, ruled over by a regent such as the [empress dowager](/wiki/Empress_dowager "Empress dowager") or one of her male relatives.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197266–72-165) Ranked immediately below the emperor were [the kings](/wiki/Kings_of_the_Han_dynasty "Kings of the Han dynasty") who were of the same [Liu](/wiki/Liu "Liu") family clan.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197276-17)[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980105–107-166) The rest of society, including [nobles](/wiki/Chinese_nobility "Chinese nobility") lower than kings and all commoners excluding slaves, belonged to one of twenty ranks (*èrshí gōngchéng* 二十公乘).

Each successive rank gave its holder greater pensions and legal privileges. The highest rank, of full [marquess](/wiki/Marquess "Marquess"), came with a state pension and a territorial [fiefdom](/wiki/Fief "Fief"). Holders of the rank immediately below, that of ordinary marquess, received a pension, but had no territorial rule.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986552–553-167)[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197216-168) [Scholar-bureaucrats](/wiki/Scholar-bureaucrat "Scholar-bureaucrat") who served in government belonged to the wider commoner social class and were ranked just below nobles in social prestige. The highest government officials could be enfeoffed as marquesses.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197284-169)

By the Eastern Han, local elites of unattached scholars, teachers, students, and government officials began to identify themselves as members of a nationwide [gentry class](/wiki/Gentry_(China) "Gentry (China)") with shared values and a commitment to mainstream scholarship.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1986631,_643–644-170)[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199980-171) When the government became noticeably corrupt in mid-to-late Eastern Han, many gentry even considered the cultivation of morally grounded personal relationships more important than serving in public office.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000141–142-140)[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007601–602-172)

Farmers, namely small landowner–cultivators, were ranked just below scholars and officials in the social hierarchy. Other agricultural cultivators were of a lower status, such as tenants, wage labourers, and slaves.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972104–111-173)[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986556–557-174)[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1986621–622-175)[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1974173–174-176) The Han dynasty made adjustments to [slavery in China](/wiki/Slavery_in_China "Slavery in China") and saw an increase in agricultural slaves. Artisans, technicians, tradespeople, and craftsmen had a legal and socioeconomic status between that of owner-cultivator farmers and common merchants.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972112-177)

State-registered merchants, who were forced by law to wear white-coloured clothes and pay high commercial taxes, were considered by the gentry as social parasites with a contemptible status.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972104–105,_119–120-178)[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986576–577-179) These were often petty shopkeepers of urban marketplaces; merchants such as industrialists and itinerant traders working between a network of cities could avoid registering as merchants and were often wealthier and more powerful than the vast majority of government officials.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986576–577-179)[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972114–117-180)

Wealthy landowners, such as nobles and officials, often provided lodging for retainers who provided valuable work or duties, sometimes including fighting bandits or riding into battle. Unlike slaves, retainers could come and go from their master's home as they pleased.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972127–128-181) Physicians, pig breeders, and butchers had fairly high social status, while occultist diviners, runners, and messengers had low status.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi2006172–173,_179–180-182)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972106,_122–127-183)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/-0202_0220_Brick_Relief_with_Acrobatic_Performance_Han_Dynasty_National_Museum_of_China_anagoria.jpg/500px--0202_0220_Brick_Relief_with_Acrobatic_Performance_Han_Dynasty_National_Museum_of_China_anagoria.jpg)](/wiki/File:-0202_0220_Brick_Relief_with_Acrobatic_Performance_Han_Dynasty_National_Museum_of_China_anagoria.jpg)

A Han dynasty brick relief with acrobats

### Marriage, gender, and kinship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Marriage, gender, and kinship")]

See also: [Women in Han China](/wiki/Women_in_ancient_and_imperial_China#Han_dynasty "Women in ancient and imperial China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Dahuting_tomb_mural_detail_of_women_wearing_hanfu%2C_Eastern_Han_period.jpg/250px-Dahuting_tomb_mural_detail_of_women_wearing_hanfu%2C_Eastern_Han_period.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dahuting_tomb_mural_detail_of_women_wearing_hanfu,_Eastern_Han_period.jpg)

Detail of an Eastern Han mural showing two women wearing *[Hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")* robes, from Dahuting, [Zhengzhou](/wiki/Zhengzhou "Zhengzhou"), Henan province

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Wall-Painting_from_a_Han_dynasty_Tomb_in_Tung-p%27ing_County.jpg/250px-Wall-Painting_from_a_Han_dynasty_Tomb_in_Tung-p%27ing_County.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wall-Painting_from_a_Han_dynasty_Tomb_in_Tung-p%27ing_County.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Queen_Mother_of_the_West_from_a_Wall-Painting_in_Han_Dynasty_Tomb.jpg/250px-Queen_Mother_of_the_West_from_a_Wall-Painting_in_Han_Dynasty_Tomb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Mother_of_the_West_from_a_Wall-Painting_in_Han_Dynasty_Tomb.jpg)

Western Han- or Xin-era murals showing men and women dressed in *[hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")*, with the [Queen Mother of the West](/wiki/Queen_Mother_of_the_West "Queen Mother of the West") dressed in *[shenyi](/wiki/Shenyi "Shenyi")*, from a tomb in [Dongping, Shandong](/w/index.php?title=Dongping,_Shandong&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dongping, Shandong (page does not exist)")

The Han-era family was [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineal "Patrilineal") and typically had four to five [nuclear family](/wiki/Nuclear_family "Nuclear family") members living in one household. Multiple generations of [extended family](/wiki/Extended_family "Extended family") members did not occupy the same house, unlike families of later dynasties.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200246–47-184)[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü19723–9-185) According to Confucian family norms, various family members were treated with different levels of respect and intimacy. For example, there were different accepted time frames for mourning the death of a father versus a paternal uncle.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü19729–10-186)

Marriages were highly ritualized, particularly for the wealthy, and included many important steps. The giving of betrothal gifts, known as [bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price") and [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry"), were especially important. A lack of either was considered dishonourable and the woman would have been seen not as a wife, but as a concubine.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiesner-Hanks201130-187) [Arranged marriages](/wiki/Arranged_marriage "Arranged marriage") were typical, with the father's input on his offspring's spouse being considered more important than the mother's.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200235-188)[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197234-189)

Monogamous marriages were also normal, although nobles and high officials were wealthy enough to afford and support concubines as additional lovers.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197244–47-190)[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200238–39-191) Under certain conditions dictated by custom, not law, both men and women were able to divorce their spouses and remarry.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200240–45-192)[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197237–43-193) However, a woman who had been widowed continued to belong to her husband's family after his death. In order to remarry, the widow would have to be returned to her family in exchange for a ransom fee. Her children would not be allowed to go with her.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiesner-Hanks201130-187)

Among the nobility, bisexuality was the norm, continuing the accepted tradition of sexual expression amongst other nobles since the Zhou dynasty.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch199035-194) In the Royal Court, Emperors often favored eunuchs above other non-castrated men for their bodies' "sexual passivity".[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch199044-195) On the other hand, Han authors did not view male homosexual individuals as effeminate, as occurred in later dynasties.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch199047-196) While non-royal nobility were obligated to heterosexual marriages, male concubines were widely accepted. Despite openness to bisexuality or homosexuality, Han dynasty norms around gender and family obligated most moral questions, including that of polygamy, homosexuality, and bisexuality, to be solved by the patriarch within the household.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch199050-197)

Apart from the passing of noble titles or ranks, [inheritance](/wiki/Inheritance "Inheritance") practices did not involve [primogeniture](/wiki/Primogeniture "Primogeniture"); each son received an equal share of the family property.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197216–17-198) Unlike the practice in later dynasties, the father usually sent his adult married sons away with their portions of the family fortune.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü19726–9-199) Daughters received a portion of the family fortune through their dowries, though this was usually much less than the shares of sons.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197217–18-200) A different distribution of the remainder could be specified in a [will](/wiki/Last_will_and_testament "Last will and testament"), but it is unclear how common this was.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197217-201)

Women were expected to obey the will of their father, then their husband, and then their adult son in old age. However, it is known from contemporary sources that there were many deviations to this rule, especially in regard to mothers over their sons, and empresses who ordered around and openly humiliated their fathers and brothers.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197249–59-202) Women were exempt from the annual [corvée](/wiki/Corv%C3%A9e "Corvée") labour duties, but often engaged in a range of income-earning occupations aside from their domestic chores of cooking and cleaning.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200274–75-203)

The most common occupation for women was weaving clothes for the family, for sale at market, or for large textile enterprises that employed hundreds of women. Other women helped on their brothers' farms or became singers, dancers, sorceresses, respected medical physicians, and successful merchants who could afford their own silk clothes.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197254–56-204)[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200229,_51,_54,_59–60,_65–68,_70–74,_77–78-205) Some women formed spinning collectives, aggregating the resources of several different families.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200229-206)

### Education, literature, and philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Education, literature, and philosophy")]

Further information: [Chinese philosophy](/wiki/Chinese_philosophy "Chinese philosophy") and [Chinese literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Confucius%2C_fresco_from_a_Western_Han_tomb_of_Dongping_County%2C_Shandong_province%2C_China.jpg/250px-Confucius%2C_fresco_from_a_Western_Han_tomb_of_Dongping_County%2C_Shandong_province%2C_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:Confucius,_fresco_from_a_Western_Han_tomb_of_Dongping_County,_Shandong_province,_China.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Confucius_and_Laozi%2C_fresco_from_a_Western_Han_tomb_of_Dongping_County%2C_Shandong_province%2C_China.jpg/250px-Confucius_and_Laozi%2C_fresco_from_a_Western_Han_tomb_of_Dongping_County%2C_Shandong_province%2C_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:Confucius_and_Laozi,_fresco_from_a_Western_Han_tomb_of_Dongping_County,_Shandong_province,_China.jpg)

A Western Han [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") depicting Confucius and [Laozi](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi"), from a tomb of [Dongping County](/wiki/Dongping_County "Dongping County"), Shandong

The early Western Han court simultaneously accepted the philosophical teachings of [Legalism](/wiki/Legalism_(Chinese_philosophy) "Legalism (Chinese philosophy)"), [Huang-Lao](/wiki/Huang-Lao "Huang-Lao") Taoism, and [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism") in making state decisions and shaping government policy.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi200624–25-207)[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1994128–130-208) However, the Han court under [Emperor Wu](/wiki/Emperor_Wu_of_Han "Emperor Wu of Han") gave Confucianism exclusive patronage. In 136 BC, he abolished all academic chairs not concerned with the [Five Classics](/wiki/Five_Classics "Five Classics"), and in 124 BC he established the [Imperial University](/wiki/Taixue "Taixue"), at which he encouraged nominees for office to receive a Confucian education.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKramers1986754–756-209)[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi20067–8-210)[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1994121–125-211)[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'en1986769-212)

Unlike the original ideology espoused by [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius") (551–479 BC), Han Confucianism in Emperor Wu's reign was the creation of [Dong Zhongshu](/wiki/Dong_Zhongshu "Dong Zhongshu") (179–104 BC). Dong was a scholar and minor official who aggregated the ethical Confucian ideas of [ritual](/wiki/Li_(Confucian) "Li (Confucian)"), [filial piety](/wiki/Filial_piety "Filial piety"), and [harmonious relationships](/wiki/Ren_(Confucianism) "Ren (Confucianism)") with [five phases](/wiki/Five_phases "Five phases") and [yin-yang](/wiki/Yin-yang "Yin-yang") cosmologies.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKramers1986753–755-213)[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1994134–140-214) Dong's synthesis justified the imperial system of government within the natural order of the universe.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKramers1986754-215)

The Imperial University grew in importance as the student body grew to over 30,000 by the 2nd century AD.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199977–78-216)[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKramers1986757-217) A Confucian-based education was also made available at commandery-level schools and [private schools](/wiki/Private_school "Private school") opened in small towns, where teachers earned respectable incomes from tuition payments.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972103-218) Schools were established in far southern regions where standard Chinese texts were used to assimilate the local populace.[[217]](#cite_note-219)

Some important texts were created and studied by scholars. Philosophy written by [Yang Xiong](/wiki/Yang_Xiong_(author) "Yang Xiong (author)") (53 BC – 18 AD), [Huan Tan](/wiki/Huan_Tan "Huan Tan") (43 BC – 28 AD), [Wang Chong](/wiki/Wang_Chong "Wang Chong") (27–100 AD), and [Wang Fu](/wiki/Wang_Fu_(philosopher) "Wang Fu (philosopher)") (78–163 AD) questioned whether human nature was innately good or evil and posed challenges to Dong's universal order.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'en1986773–794-220) The *[Shiji](/wiki/Shiji "Shiji")* started by [Sima Tan](/wiki/Sima_Tan "Sima Tan") (d. 110 BC) and finished by his son [Sima Qian](/wiki/Sima_Qian "Sima Qian") (145–86 BC) established the [standard model](/wiki/Chinese_historiography "Chinese historiography") for imperial China's [tradition of official histories](/wiki/Twenty-Four_Histories "Twenty-Four Histories"), being emulated initially by the *[Book of Han](/wiki/Book_of_Han "Book of Han")* authored by [Ban Biao](/wiki/Ban_Biao "Ban Biao") (3–54 AD) with his son [Ban Gu](/wiki/Ban_Gu "Ban Gu") (32–92 AD), and his daughter [Ban Zhao](/wiki/Ban_Zhao "Ban Zhao") (45–116 AD).[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardy199914–15-221)[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000137–138-222) [Biographies](/wiki/Biographies "Biographies") on important figures were written by members of the gentry.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1986645-223) There were also [dictionaries](/wiki/Chinese_dictionaries "Chinese dictionaries") published during the Han period such as the *[Shuowen Jiezi](/wiki/Shuowen_Jiezi "Shuowen Jiezi")* by [Xu Shen](/wiki/Xu_Shen "Xu Shen") (c. 58 – c. 147 AD) and the *[Fangyan](/wiki/Fangyan_(book) "Fangyan (book)")* by [Yang Xiong](/wiki/Yang_Xiong_(author) "Yang Xiong (author)").[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988185-224)[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEXue2003161-225) Han dynasty poetry was dominated by the [*fu* genre](/wiki/Fu_(poetry) "Fu (poetry)"), which achieved its greatest prominence during the reign of Emperor Wu.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000137–138-222)[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071049-226)[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeinhauserHartmanMaWest1986212-227)[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis2007222-228)[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECutter198925–26-229)

### Law and order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Law and order")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Mawangdui_silk_banner_from_tomb_no1.jpg/250px-Mawangdui_silk_banner_from_tomb_no1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mawangdui_silk_banner_from_tomb_no1.jpg)

A silk banner from [Mawangdui](/wiki/Mawangdui "Mawangdui"), draped over the coffin of [Lady Dai](/wiki/Lady_Dai "Lady Dai") (d. 168 BC), wife of the Marquess Li Cang (利蒼) (d. 186 BC), chancellor for the [Kingdom of Changsha](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Changsha "Kingdom of Changsha").[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000117–119-230)

Han scholars such as [Jia Yi](/wiki/Jia_Yi "Jia Yi") (201–169 BC) portrayed the Qin as a brutal regime.
