However, archaeological evidence from [Zhangjiashan](/wiki/Zhangjiashan "Zhangjiashan") and [Shuihudi](/wiki/Shuihudi "Shuihudi") reveal that many of the statutes in the Han law code compiled by Chancellor [Xiao He](/wiki/Xiao_He "Xiao He") (d. 193 BC) were derived from Qin law.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHulsewé1986525–526-231)[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi200623–24-232)[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000110–112-233)

Various cases for rape, physical abuse, and murder were prosecuted in court. Women, although usually having fewer rights by custom, were allowed to level civil and criminal charges against men.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHulsewé1986523–530-234)[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200282-235) While suspects were jailed, convicted criminals were never imprisoned. Instead, punishments were commonly monetary fines, periods of forced hard labour for convicts, and the penalty of death by beheading.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHulsewé1986532–535-236) Early Han punishments of torturous mutilation were borrowed from Qin law. A series of reforms abolished mutilation punishments with progressively less-severe beatings by the [bastinado](/wiki/Bastinado "Bastinado").[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHulsewé1986531–533-237)

Acting as a judge in lawsuits was one of the many duties of the [county magistrate](/wiki/County_magistrate "County magistrate") and Administrators of commanderies. Complex, high-profile, or unresolved cases were often deferred to the Minister of Justice in the capital or even the emperor.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHulsewé1986528–529-238) In each Han county was several districts, each overseen by a chief of police. Order in the cities was maintained by government officers in the marketplaces and [constables](/wiki/Constable "Constable") in the neighbourhoods.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986552–553,_576-239)[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1968146–147-240)

### Food

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Food")]

The most common staple crops consumed during Han were [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley"), [foxtail millet](/wiki/Foxtail_millet "Foxtail millet"), [proso millet](/wiki/Proso_millet "Proso millet"), rice, and [beans](/wiki/Bean "Bean").[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang198252-241) Commonly eaten fruits and vegetables included chestnuts, pears, plums, peaches, melons, apricots, strawberries, [red bayberries](/wiki/Myrica "Myrica"), [jujubes](/wiki/Jujube "Jujube"), [calabash](/wiki/Calabash "Calabash"), [bamboo shoots](/wiki/Bamboo_shoot "Bamboo shoot"), [mustard plant](/wiki/Mustard_plant "Mustard plant"), and [taro](/wiki/Taro "Taro").[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang198253,_206-242) Domesticated animals that were also eaten included chickens, [Mandarin ducks](/wiki/Mandarin_duck "Mandarin duck"), geese, cows, sheep, pigs, camels, and dogs (various types were bred specifically for food, while most were used as pets). Turtles and fish were taken from streams and lakes. Commonly hunted game, such as owl, pheasant, magpie, [sika deer](/wiki/Sika_deer "Sika deer"), and [Chinese bamboo partridge](/wiki/Chinese_bamboo_partridge "Chinese bamboo partridge") were consumed.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang198257–58-243) Seasoning included sugar, honey, salt, and [soy sauce](/wiki/Soy_sauce "Soy sauce").[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000119–121-244) Beer and wine were regularly consumed.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1982206-245)[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000119-246)

### Clothing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Clothing")]

Further information: [Hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")

The types of clothing worn and the materials used during the Han period depended upon social class. Wealthy folk could afford silk robes, skirts, socks, and mittens, coats made of badger or fox fur, duck plumes, and [slippers](/wiki/Slippers "Slippers") with inlaid leather, pearls, and silk lining. Peasants commonly wore clothes made of [hemp](/wiki/Hemp "Hemp"), [wool](/wiki/Wool "Wool"), and [ferret](/wiki/Ferret "Ferret") skins.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang198253,_59–63,_206-247)[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1968139-248)[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972128-249)

### Religion, cosmology, and metaphysics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Religion, cosmology, and metaphysics")]

Further information: [Chinese mythology](/wiki/Chinese_mythology "Chinese mythology"), [Chinese theology](/wiki/Chinese_theology "Chinese theology"), and [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Bronze_Chimera%2C_Eastern_Han_Dynasty.jpg/250px-Bronze_Chimera%2C_Eastern_Han_Dynasty.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bronze_Chimera,_Eastern_Han_Dynasty.jpg)

An Eastern Han bronze statuette of a *[qilin](/wiki/Qilin "Qilin")*, 1st century AD

Families throughout Han China made ritual sacrifices of animals and food to deities, spirits, and ancestors at [temples and shrines](/wiki/Temple_(Chinese) "Temple (Chinese)"). They believed that these items could be used by those in the spiritual realm.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197230–31-250) It was thought that each person had a [two-part soul](/wiki/Hun_and_po "Hun and po"): the spirit-soul which journeyed to the afterlife paradise of immortals (*[xian](/wiki/Xian_(Taoism) "Xian (Taoism)")*), and the body-soul which remained in its grave or tomb on earth and was only reunited with the spirit-soul through a ritual ceremony.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000119-246)[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi2006140–141-251)

In addition to his many other roles, the emperor acted as the highest priest in the land who made sacrifices to Heaven, the [main deities](/wiki/Chinese_mythology "Chinese mythology") known as the [Five Powers](/wiki/Three_Sovereigns_and_Five_Emperors "Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors"), and spirits of mountains and rivers known as *[shen](/wiki/Shen_(Chinese_religion) "Shen (Chinese religion)")*.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197271-252) It was believed that the three realms of Heaven, Earth, and Mankind were linked by natural cycles of [yin and yang](/wiki/Yin_and_yang "Yin and yang") and the [five phases](/wiki/Five_phases "Five phases").[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe199455-253)[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi2006167-254)[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESunKistemaker19972–3-255)[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199978–79-256) If the emperor did not behave according to proper ritual, ethics, and morals, he could disrupt the fine balance of these cosmological cycles and cause calamities such as earthquakes, floods, droughts, epidemics, and swarms of locusts.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199978–79-256)[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986201-257)[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007496,_592-258)

It was believed that immortality could be achieved if one reached the lands of the [Queen Mother of the West](/wiki/Queen_Mother_of_the_West "Queen Mother of the West") or [Mount Penglai](/wiki/Mount_Penglai "Mount Penglai").[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe2005101–102-259)[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi2006116–117-260) Han-era Taoists assembled into small groups of hermits who attempted to achieve immortality through breathing exercises, sexual techniques, and the use of medical elixirs.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000144-261)

By the 2nd century AD, Taoists formed large hierarchical religious societies such as the [Way of the Five Pecks of Rice](/wiki/Way_of_the_Five_Pecks_of_Rice "Way of the Five Pecks of Rice"). Its followers believed that the sage-philosopher [Laozi](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi") (fl. 6th century BC) was a holy prophet who would offer [salvation](/wiki/Salvation "Salvation") and good health if his devout followers would confess their sins, ban the worship of unclean gods who accepted meat sacrifices, and chant sections of the *[Tao Te Ching](/wiki/Tao_Te_Ching "Tao Te Ching")*.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000144–146-262)

Buddhism first entered Imperial China [through the Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road_transmission_of_Buddhism "Silk Road transmission of Buddhism") during the Eastern Han, and was first mentioned in 65 AD.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1972112-263)[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemiéville1986821–822-264) [Liu Ying](/wiki/Liu_Ying_(prince) "Liu Ying (prince)") (d. 71 AD), a half-brother to [Emperor Ming of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Ming_of_Han "Emperor Ming of Han") (r. 57–75 AD), was one of its earliest Chinese adherents, although [Chinese Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism") at this point was heavily associated with Huang–Lao Taoism.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemiéville1986821–822-264) China's first known Buddhist temple, the [White Horse Temple](/wiki/White_Horse_Temple "White Horse Temple"), was constructed outside the wall of Luoyang during Emperor Ming's reign.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemiéville1986823-265) Important Buddhist canons were translated into Chinese during the 2nd century AD, including the *[Sutra of Forty-two Chapters](/wiki/Sutra_of_Forty-two_Chapters "Sutra of Forty-two Chapters")*, *[Perfection of Wisdom](/wiki/Perfection_of_Wisdom "Perfection of Wisdom")*, *[Shurangama Sutra](/wiki/Shurangama_Sutra "Shurangama Sutra")*, and *[Pratyutpanna Sutra](/wiki/Pratyutpanna_Sutra "Pratyutpanna Sutra")*.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkira1998247–251-266)[[c]](#cite_note-267)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main article: [Government of the Han dynasty](/wiki/Government_of_the_Han_dynasty "Government of the Han dynasty")

See also: [List of emperors of the Han dynasty](/wiki/List_of_emperors_of_the_Han_dynasty "List of emperors of the Han dynasty")

### Central government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Central government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Pantings_on_Lacquar_Basket_Lo-lang.jpg/250px-Pantings_on_Lacquar_Basket_Lo-lang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pantings_on_Lacquar_Basket_Lo-lang.jpg)

A scene of historic paragons of filial piety conversing with one another, Chinese painted artwork on a [lacquerware](/wiki/Lacquerware "Lacquerware") basketwork box, excavated from a tomb of the [Lelang Commandery](/wiki/Lelang_Commandery "Lelang Commandery") on the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula") dating to the Eastern Han.

In Han government, the emperor was the supreme judge and lawgiver, the commander-in-chief of the armed forces and sole designator of official nominees appointed to the top posts in central and local administrations; those who earned a [600-bushel salary-rank or higher](/wiki/Government_of_the_Han_dynasty#Salaries "Government of the Han dynasty").[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071216-268)[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1949141–143-269) Theoretically, there were no limits to his power.

However, state organs with competing interests and institutions such as the court conference (*tingyi* 廷議)—where ministers were convened to reach a majority consensus on an issue—pressured the emperor to accept the advice of his ministers on policy decisions.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980144-270)[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1949173–177-271) If the emperor rejected a court conference decision, he risked alienating his high ministers. Nevertheless, emperors sometimes did reject the majority opinion reached at court conferences.[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197270–71-272)

Below the emperor were his [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)") members known as the [Three Councillors of State](/wiki/Three_Councillors_of_State "Three Councillors of State"). These were the [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_China "Chancellor of China") or [Minister over the Masses](/wiki/Minister_over_the_Masses "Minister over the Masses"), the Imperial Counsellor or Excellency of Works (*Yushi dafu* 御史大夫 or *Da sikong* 大司空), and Grand Commandant or Grand Marshal (*Taiwei* 太尉 or *Da sima* 大司馬).[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071221-273)[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein19807–17-274)

The Chancellor, whose title had changed in 8 BC to Minister over the Masses, was chiefly responsible for drafting the [government budget](/wiki/Government_budget "Government budget"). The Chancellor's other duties included managing provincial registers for land and population, leading court conferences, acting as judge in lawsuits, and recommending nominees for high office. He could appoint officials below the salary-rank of 600 bushels.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1949143–144,_145–146,_177-275)[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein19807–8,_14-276)

The Imperial Counsellor's chief duty was to conduct disciplinary procedures for officials. He shared similar duties with the Chancellor, such as receiving annual provincial reports. However, when his title was changed to Minister of Works in 8 BC, his chief duty became the oversight of public works projects.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1949147–148-277)[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein19808–9,_15–16-278)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Rubbing_of_a_Han_Citang.JPG/250px-Rubbing_of_a_Han_Citang.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rubbing_of_a_Han_Citang.JPG)

A rubbing of a Han pictorial stone showing an [ancestor worship](/wiki/Ancestor_worship "Ancestor worship") hall (*cítang* 祠堂)

The Grand Commandant, whose title was changed to Grand Marshal in 119 BC before reverting to Grand Commandant in 51 AD, was the irregularly posted commander of the military and then regent during the Western Han period. In the Eastern Han era he was chiefly a civil official who shared many of the same censorial powers as the other two Councillors of State.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1949150-279)[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198010–13-280)

Ranked below the Three Councillors of State were the [Nine Ministers](/wiki/Nine_Ministers "Nine Ministers"), who each headed a specialized ministry. The Minister of Ceremonies (*Taichang* 太常) was the chief official in charge of religious rites, rituals, prayers, and the maintenance of ancestral temples and altars.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071222-281)[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1949151-282)[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198017–23-283) The Minister of the Household (*Guang lu xun* 光祿勳) was in charge of the emperor's security within the palace grounds, external imperial parks, and wherever the emperor made an outing by chariot.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071222-281)[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198023–24-284)

The Minister of the Guards (*Weiwei* 衛尉) was responsible for securing and patrolling the walls, towers, and gates of the imperial palaces.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071223-285)[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198031-286) The Minister Coachman (*Taipu* 太僕) was responsible for the maintenance of imperial stables, horses, carriages, and coach-houses for the emperor and his palace attendants, as well as the supply of horses for the armed forces.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071223-285)[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198034–35-287) The Minister of Justice (*Tingwei* 廷尉) was the chief official in charge of upholding, administering, and interpreting the law.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198038-288)[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1949154-289) The Minister Herald (*Da honglu* 大鴻臚) was the chief official in charge of receiving honoured guests like nobles and [foreign ambassadors](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Imperial_China "Foreign relations of Imperial China") at court.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071223–1224-290)[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198039–40-291)

The Minister of the Imperial Clan (*Zongzheng* 宗正) oversaw the imperial court's interactions with the empire's nobility and extended imperial family, such as granting fiefs and titles.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1949155-292)[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198041-293) The Minister of Finance (*da sìnong* 大司農) was the [treasurer](/wiki/Treasurer "Treasurer") for the official bureaucracy and the armed forces who handled tax revenues and set standards for units of measurement.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071224-294)[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198043-295) The Minister Steward (*Shaofu* 少府) served the emperor exclusively, providing him with entertainment and amusements, proper food and clothing, medicine and physical care, valuables and equipment.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071224-294)[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198047-296)

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Local government")]

See also: [List of provinces and commanderies of the Han dynasty](/wiki/List_of_provinces_and_commanderies_of_the_Han_dynasty "List of provinces and commanderies of the Han dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Li_Bing_Statue_2.JPG/250px-Li_Bing_Statue_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Li_Bing_Statue_2.JPG)

An Eastern Han devotional stone statue depicting [Li Bing](/wiki/Li_Bing_(Qin) "Li Bing (Qin)") (fl. 3rd century BC) in an official's cap and robe in [Dujiangyan](/wiki/Dujiangyan "Dujiangyan"), Sichuan

The Han empire, excluding kingdoms and marquessates, was divided, in descending order of size, into political units of [provinces](/wiki/Zhou_(country_subdivision) "Zhou (country subdivision)"), [commanderies](/wiki/Commandery_(China) "Commandery (China)"), and [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_China#History "Counties of China").[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071228-297) A county was divided into several districts (*xiang* 鄉), the latter composed of a group of [hamlets](/wiki/Hamlets "Hamlets") (*li* 里), each containing about a hundred families.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980103-298)[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986551–552-299)

The heads of provinces, whose official title was changed from Inspector to Governor and vice versa several times during Han, were responsible for inspecting several commandery-level and kingdom-level administrations.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198090–92-300)[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1949158–160-301) On the basis of their reports, the officials in these local administrations would be promoted, demoted, dismissed, or prosecuted by the imperial court.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198091-302)

A governor could take various actions without permission from the imperial court. The lower-ranked inspector had executive powers only during times of crisis, such as raising militias across the commanderies under his jurisdiction to suppress a rebellion.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071228-297)

A commandery consisted of a group of counties, and was headed by an administrator.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071228-297) He was the top civil and military leader of the commandery and handled defence, lawsuits, seasonal instructions to farmers, and recommendations of nominees for office sent annually to the capital in a quota system first established by Emperor Wu.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071230–1231-303)[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198096-304)[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHsu1965367–368-305) The head of a large county of about 10,000 households was called a Prefect, while the heads of smaller counties were called chiefs, and both could be referred to as [magistrates](/wiki/Magistrate "Magistrate").[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071230-306)[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980100-307) A Magistrate maintained law and order in his county, registered the populace for taxation, mobilized commoners for annual [corvée](/wiki/Corv%C3%A9e "Corvée") duties, repaired schools, and supervised public works.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980100-307)

### Kingdoms and marquessates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Kingdoms and marquessates")]

Main article: [Kings of the Han dynasty](/wiki/Kings_of_the_Han_dynasty "Kings of the Han dynasty")

Kingdoms—roughly the size of [commanderies](/wiki/Commandery_(China) "Commandery (China)")—were ruled exclusively by the emperor's male relatives as semi-autonomous [fiefdoms](/wiki/Fiefdom "Fiefdom"). Before 157 BC, some kingdoms were ruled by non-relatives, granted to them in return for their services to Emperor Gaozu. The administration of each kingdom was very similar to that of the central government.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHsu1965360-308)[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980105–106-309)[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986126-310) Although the emperor appointed the Chancellor of each kingdom, kings appointed all the remaining civil officials in their fiefs.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHsu1965360-308)[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980105–106-309)

However, in 145 BC, after several insurrections by the kings, Emperor Jing removed the kings' rights to appoint officials whose [salaries were higher than 400 bushels](/wiki/Government_of_the_Han_dynasty#Salaries "Government of the Han dynasty").[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980105–106-309) The Imperial Counsellors and Nine Ministers (excluding the Minister Coachman) of every kingdom were abolished, although the Chancellor was still appointed by the central government.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980105–106-309)

With these reforms, kings were reduced to being nominal heads of their fiefs, gaining a personal income from only a portion of the taxes collected in their kingdom.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197276-17) Similarly, the officials in the administrative staff of a full marquess's fief were appointed by the central government. A marquess's chancellor was ranked as the equivalent of a county prefect. Like a king, the marquess collected a portion of the tax revenues in his fief as personal income.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071230-306)[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980108-311)

Until the reign of [Emperor Jing of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Jing_of_Han "Emperor Jing of Han"), the Han emperors had great difficulties controlling their vassal kings, who often switched allegiances to the [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu") whenever they felt threatened by imperial centralization of power. The seven years of Gaozu's reign featured defections by three vassal kings and one marquess, who then aligned themselves with the Xiongnu. Even imperial princes controlling fiefdoms would sometimes invite a Xiongnu invasion in response to the Emperor's threats. The Han moved to secure a treaty with the Xiongnu, aiming to clearly divide authority between them. The Han and Xiongnu now held one another out as the "two masters" with sole dominion over their respective peoples; they cemented this agreement with a marriage alliance (*[heqin](/wiki/Heqin "Heqin")*), before eliminating the rebellious vassal kings in 154 BC. This prompted some of the Xiongnu vassals to swap allegiances to the Han, starting in 147. Han court officials were initially hostile to the idea of disrupting the status quo by expanding into Xiongnu territory in the steppe. The surrendered Xiongnu were integrated into a parallel military and political structures loyal to the Han emperor, a step toward a potential Han challenge to the superiority of Xiongnu cavalry in steppe warfare. This also brought the Han into contact with the interstate trade networks through the Tarim Basin in the far northwest, allowing for the Han's expansion from a regional state to a universalist, cosmopolitan empire achieved in part through further marriage alliances with the [Wusun](/wiki/Wusun "Wusun"), another steppe power.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisHsieh201732–39-312)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Military")]

Main article: [Military of the Han dynasty](/wiki/Military_of_the_Han_dynasty "Military of the Han dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/04/Dahuting_Tomb_mural%2C_cavalry_and_chariots%2C_Eastern_Han_Dynasty.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dahuting_Tomb_mural,_cavalry_and_chariots,_Eastern_Han_Dynasty.jpg)

A mural showing [chariots and cavalry](/wiki/Horses_in_East_Asian_warfare "Horses in East Asian warfare") from the [Dahuting tomb](/wiki/Dahuting_tomb "Dahuting tomb") near [Zhengzhou](/wiki/Zhengzhou "Zhengzhou"), Henan, dated to the late Eastern Han

At the beginning of the Han, every male commoner aged twenty-three was liable for [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") into the military. The minimum age was reduced to twenty following the reign of [Emperor Zhao](/wiki/Emperor_Zhao "Emperor Zhao") (r. 87–74).[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChang200770–71-313) Conscripted soldiers underwent one year of training and one year of service as non-professional soldiers. The year of training was spent in one of three branches of the armed forces: [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"), [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry"), or [navy](/wiki/Navy "Navy"). Prior to the abolition of much of the conscription system after 30 AD, soldiers could be called up for future service following the completion of their terms. They had to continue training regularly to maintain their skills, and were subject to annual inspections of their military readiness.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986599-314)[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980114-315) The year of active service was served either on the frontier, in a king's court, or in the capital under the Minister of the Guards. A small professional army was stationed near the capital.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986599-314)[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980114-315)

During the Eastern Han, conscription could be avoided if one paid a commutable tax. The Eastern Han court favoured the recruitment of a volunteer army.[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007564–565,_1234-316) The volunteer army comprised the Southern Army (*Nanjun* 南軍), while the [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") stationed in and near the capital was the Northern Army (*Beijun* 北軍).[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980114–115-317) Led by Colonels (*Xiaowei* 校尉), the Northern Army consisted of five regiments, each composed of several thousand soldiers.[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071234-318)[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980117–118-319) When central authority collapsed after 189 AD, wealthy landowners, members of the aristocracy/nobility, and regional military-governors relied upon their retainers to act as their own personal troops.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972132–133-320)

During times of war, the volunteer army was increased, and a much larger militia was raised across the country to supplement the Northern Army. In these circumstances, a general (*jiangjun* 將軍) led a [division](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)"), which was divided into [regiments](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment") led by a colonel or major (*sima* 司馬). Regiments were divided into [companies](/wiki/Company_(military_unit) "Company (military unit)") and led by captains. [Platoons](/wiki/Platoon "Platoon") were the smallest units.[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071234-318)[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1980116,_120–122-321)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of the Han dynasty](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Han_dynasty "Economy of the Han dynasty")

### Currency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Currency")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/S-114_W_Han_wuzhu%2C_Han_Wudi%2C_140-87%2C_25_5mm.jpg/250px-S-114_W_Han_wuzhu%2C_Han_Wudi%2C_140-87%2C_25_5mm.jpg)](/wiki/File:S-114_W_Han_wuzhu,_Han_Wudi,_140-87,_25_5mm.jpg)

A *wuzhu* (五銖) coin issued during the reign of [Emperor Wu](/wiki/Emperor_Wu_of_Han "Emperor Wu of Han") (r. 141–87 BC), 25.5 mm in diameter

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/%E9%87%91%E9%A5%BC_%E6%B5%B7%E6%98%8F%E4%BE%AF%E5%9B%BD%E9%81%97%E5%9D%80.JPG/250px-%E9%87%91%E9%A5%BC_%E6%B5%B7%E6%98%8F%E4%BE%AF%E5%9B%BD%E9%81%97%E5%9D%80.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E9%87%91%E9%A5%BC_%E6%B5%B7%E6%98%8F%E4%BE%AF%E5%9B%BD%E9%81%97%E5%9D%80.JPG)

Gold coins from the Eastern Han

The Han dynasty inherited the *[ban liang](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_coinage#Ban_Liang_coins "Ancient Chinese coinage")* coin type from the Qin. In the beginning of the Han, Emperor Gaozu closed the [government mint](/wiki/Mint_(coin) "Mint (coin)") in favour of private minting of coins. This decision was reversed in 186 BC by his widow [Grand Empress Dowager Lü Zhi](/wiki/Empress_L%C3%BC_Zhi "Empress Lü Zhi") (d. 180 BC), who abolished private minting.[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986586-322) In 182 BC, Lü Zhi issued a bronze coin that was much lighter in weight than previous coins. This caused widespread [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") that was not reduced until 175 BC, when Emperor Wen allowed private minters to manufacture coins that were precisely 2.6 g (0.092 oz) in weight.[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986586-322)

In 144 BC, Emperor Jing abolished private minting in favour of central-government and commandery-level minting; he also introduced a new coin.[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986586–587-323) Emperor Wu introduced another in 120 BC, but a year later he abandoned the *ban liangs* entirely in favour of the *[wuzhu](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_coinage#Western_Han_and_the_Wu_Zhu_coins "Ancient Chinese coinage")* coin, weighing 3.2 g (0.11 oz).[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986587-324) The *wuzhu* became China's standard coin until the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (618–907). Its use was interrupted briefly by several new currencies introduced during Wang Mang's regime until it was reinstated in 40 AD by Emperor Guangwu.[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1986609-325)[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein1986232–233-326)[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986587–588-327)

Since commandery-issued coins were often of inferior quality and lighter weight, the central government closed commandery mints and monopolized the issue of coinage in 113 BC. This central government issuance of coinage was overseen by the [Superintendent of Waterways and Parks](/wiki/Government_of_the_Han_dynasty#Superintendent_of_Waterways_and_Parks "Government of the Han dynasty"), this duty being transferred to the Minister of Finance during the Eastern Han.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986587–588-327)[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBielenstein198047,_83-328)

### Taxation and property

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Taxation and property")]

Aside from the landowner's [land tax](/wiki/Land_value_tax#History "Land value tax") paid in a portion of their [crop yield](/wiki/Crop_yield "Crop yield"), the [poll tax](/wiki/Poll_tax "Poll tax") and [property taxes](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax") were paid in coin cash.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986600–601-329) The annual poll tax rate for adult men and women was 120 coins and 20 coins for minors. Merchants were required to pay a higher rate of 240 coins.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986598-330) The poll tax stimulated a money economy that necessitated the minting of over 28,000,000,000 coins from 118 BC to 5 AD, an average of 220,000,000 coins a year.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986588-331)

The widespread circulation of coin cash allowed successful merchants to invest money in land, empowering the very social class the government attempted to suppress through heavy commercial and property taxes.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986601-332) Emperor Wu even enacted laws which banned registered merchants from owning land, yet powerful merchants were able to avoid registration and own large tracts of land.[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986577-333)[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü1972113–114-334)

The small landowner-cultivators formed the majority of the Han tax base; this revenue was threatened during the latter half of Eastern Han when many peasants fell into debt and were forced to work as farming tenants for wealthy landlords.[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986558–601-335)[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1974173_174-336)[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199974–75-337) The Han government enacted reforms in order to keep small landowner-cultivators out of debt and on their own farms. These reforms included reducing taxes, temporary remissions of taxes, granting loans, and providing landless peasants temporary lodging and work in [agricultural colonies](/wiki/Tuntian "Tuntian") until they could recover from their debts.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199975-63)[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1986619–621-338)

In 168 BC, the land tax rate was reduced from one-fifteenth of a farming household's crop yield to one-thirtieth,[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1986149–150-339)[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986596–598-340) and later to one-hundredth of a crop yield for the last decades of the dynasty. The consequent loss of government revenue was compensated for by increasing property taxes.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986596–598-340)

The labour tax took the form of conscripted labour for one month per year, which was imposed upon male commoners aged fifteen to fifty-six. This could be avoided in Eastern Han with a commutable tax, since hired labour became more popular.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986599-314)[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007564–565-341)

### Private manufacture and government monopolies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Private manufacture and government monopolies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Western_Han_Iron_Halberd_%26_Dagger_%289930184036%29.jpg/250px-Western_Han_Iron_Halberd_%26_Dagger_%289930184036%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Western_Han_Iron_Halberd_%26_Dagger_(9930184036).jpg)

An iron *[ji](/wiki/Ji_(polearm) "Ji (polearm)")* polearm and iron dagger

In the early Western Han, a wealthy salt or iron industrialist, whether a semi-autonomous king or wealthy merchant, could boast funds that rivalled the imperial treasury and amass a peasant workforce numbering in the thousands. This kept many peasants away from their farms and denied the government a significant portion of its land tax revenue.[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c22-342)[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986583–584-343) To eliminate the influence of such private entrepreneurs, Emperor Wu nationalized the salt and iron industries in 117 BC and allowed many of the former industrialists to become officials administering the state monopolies.[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986584-344)[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWagner20011–2-345)[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200221–22-346) By the Eastern Han, the central government monopolies were repealed in favour of production by commandery and county administrations, as well as private businessmen.[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986584-344)[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWagner200115–17-347)

[Liquor](/wiki/Liquor "Liquor") was another profitable private industry nationalized by the central government in 98 BC. However, this was repealed in 81 BC and a property tax rate of two coins for every 0.2 litres (0.053 US gal) was levied for those who traded it privately.[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986600-348)[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWagner200113–14-349) By 110 BC, Emperor Wu also interfered with the profitable trade in grain when he eliminated speculation by selling government stores of grain at a lower price than that demanded by merchants.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199975-63) Apart from Emperor Ming's creation of a short-lived Office for Price Adjustment and Stabilization, which was abolished in 68 AD, central-government price control regulations were largely absent during the Eastern Han.[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007605-350)

## Science and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Science and technology")]

Main article: [Science and technology of the Han dynasty](/wiki/Science_and_technology_of_the_Han_dynasty "Science and technology of the Han dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/ChangXingongdeng.jpg/250px-ChangXingongdeng.jpg)](/wiki/File:ChangXingongdeng.jpg)

A gilded bronze [oil lamp](/wiki/Oil_lamp "Oil lamp") in the shape of a kneeling female servant, dated to the 2nd century BC, found in the tomb of [Dou Wan](/wiki/Dou_Wan "Dou Wan"), wife of [Liu Sheng, Prince of Zhongshan](/wiki/Liu_Sheng,_Prince_of_Zhongshan "Liu Sheng, Prince of Zhongshan"); its sliding shutter allows for adjustments in the direction and brightness in light while it also traps smoke within the body.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199966-75)[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1982100-351)

The Han dynasty was a unique period in the development of premodern Chinese science and technology, comparable to the level of [scientific and technological growth](/wiki/Technology_of_the_Song_dynasty "Technology of the Song dynasty") during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") (960–1279).[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJinFanLiu1996178–179-352)[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1972111-353)

### Writing materials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Writing materials")]

In the 1st millennium BC, typical ancient Chinese writing materials were [bronzeware](/wiki/Chinese_bronze_inscriptions "Chinese bronze inscriptions"), [oracle bones](/wiki/Oracle_bone_script "Oracle bone script"), and [bamboo slips](/wiki/Bamboo_slip "Bamboo slip") or wooden boards. By the beginning of the Han, the chief writing materials were [clay tablets](/wiki/Clay_tablet "Clay tablet"), silk cloth, hemp [paper](/wiki/History_of_paper#Paper_in_China "History of paper"),[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedhamTsien198638-354)[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi1974-355) and rolled scrolls made from bamboo strips sewn together with hempen string; these were passed through drilled holes and secured with clay stamps.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe196889,_94–95-356)[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETom198999-357)[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECotterell200411–13-358)

The oldest known Chinese piece of hempen paper dates to the 2nd century BC.[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuisseret199812-359)[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedhamTsien198638-354) The standard papermaking process was invented by [Cai Lun](/wiki/Cai_Lun "Cai Lun") (AD 50–121) in 105.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedhamTsien19861–2,_40–41,_122–123,_228-360)[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDayMcNeil1996122-361) The oldest known surviving piece of paper with writing on it was found in the ruins of a Han [watchtower](/wiki/Watchtower "Watchtower") that had been abandoned in AD 110, in [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia").[[359]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECotterell200411-362)

### Metallurgy and agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Metallurgy and agriculture")]

Evidence suggests that [blast furnaces](/wiki/Blast_furnace "Blast furnace"), that convert raw [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore") into [pig iron](/wiki/Pig_iron "Pig iron"), which can be remelted in a [cupola furnace](/wiki/Cupola_furnace "Cupola furnace") to produce [cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") by means of a [cold blast](/wiki/Cold_blast "Cold blast") and [hot blast](/wiki/Hot_blast "Hot blast"), were operational in China by the late [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period") (c. 770 – c. 481 BC).[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWagner20017,_36–37,_64–68,_75–76-363)[[361]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPigott1999183–184-364) The [bloomery](/wiki/Bloomery "Bloomery") was non-existent in ancient China; however, the Han-era Chinese produced [wrought iron](/wiki/Wrought_iron "Wrought iron") by injecting excess oxygen into a furnace and causing [decarburisation](/wiki/Decarburisation "Decarburisation").[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPigott1999177,_191-365) Cast iron and pig iron could be converted into wrought iron and steel using a [fining](/wiki/Finery_forge "Finery forge") process.[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1982125-366)[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPigott1999186-367)

The Han dynasty Chinese used bronze and iron to make a range of weapons, culinary tools, carpenters' tools, and domestic wares.[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWagner1993336-368)[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1982103–105,_122–124-369) A significant product of these improved iron-smelting techniques was the manufacture of new agricultural tools. The three-legged iron [seed drill](/wiki/Seed_drill "Seed drill"), invented by the 2nd century BC, enabled farmers to carefully plant crops in rows instead of [sowing](/wiki/Sowing "Sowing") seeds by hand.[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenberger200612-370)[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECotterell200424-371)[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang198254–55-372) The heavy mouldboard iron plough, also invented during the Han, required only one man to control it with two oxen to pull it. It had three [ploughshares](/wiki/Ploughshare "Ploughshare"), a seed box for the drills, a tool which turned down the soil and could sow roughly 45,730 m2 (492,200 sq ft) of land in a single day.[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986563–564-373)[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1986616–617-374)

To protect crops from wind and drought, the grain intendant Zhao Guo (趙過) created the alternating fields system (*daitianfa* 代田法) during Emperor Wu's reign. This system switched the positions of furrows and ridges between growing seasons.[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986561–563-375) Once experiments with this system yielded successful results, the government officially sponsored it and encouraged peasants to use it.[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986561–563-375) Han farmers also used the pit field system (*aotian* 凹田) for growing crops, which involved heavily fertilized pits that did not require ploughs or oxen and could be placed on sloping terrain.[[373]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsch200267–68-376)[[374]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986564–566-377) In the southern and small parts of central Han-era China, [paddy fields](/wiki/Paddy_field "Paddy field") were chiefly used to grow rice, while farmers along the [Huai River](/wiki/Huai_River "Huai River") used [transplantation](/wiki/Transplanting "Transplanting") methods of rice production.[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986568–572-378)

### Structural and geo-technical engineering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Structural and geo-technical engineering")]

Further information: [Han dynasty tomb architecture](/wiki/Han_dynasty_tomb_architecture "Han dynasty tomb architecture") and [Western Han dynasty imperial tombs](/wiki/Western_Han_dynasty_imperial_tombs "Western Han dynasty imperial tombs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Han_pottery_manor_02.jpg/250px-Han_pottery_manor_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Han_pottery_manor_02.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/%E4%B8%83%E5%B1%82%E8%BF%9E%E9%98%81%E7%BB%98%E5%BD%A9%E9%99%B6%E6%A5%BC1394.jpg/250px-%E4%B8%83%E5%B1%82%E8%BF%9E%E9%98%81%E7%BB%98%E5%BD%A9%E9%99%B6%E6%A5%BC1394.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E4%B8%83%E5%B1%82%E8%BF%9E%E9%98%81%E7%BB%98%E5%BD%A9%E9%99%B6%E6%A5%BC1394.jpg)

**Left**: A pottery model of a palace from a Han dynasty tomb; the entrances to the emperor's palaces were strictly guarded by the Minister of the Guards; if it was found that a commoner, official, or noble entered without explicit permission via a tally system, the intruder was subject to execution.[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECh'ü197268–69-379)  
 **Right**: A painted ceramic architectural model depicting a fortified manor with towers, a courtyard, [verandas](/wiki/Veranda "Veranda"), tiled rooftops, *[dougong](/wiki/Dougong "Dougong")* support brackets, and a [covered bridge](/wiki/Covered_bridge "Covered bridge") extending from the third floor of the main tower to the smaller watchtower, found in an Eastern Han tomb at Jiazuo, Henan[[377]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuo200546–48-380)

Timber was the chief building material during the Han; it was used to build palace halls, multi-story residential towers and halls, and single-story houses.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199976-381) Because wood decays rapidly, the only remaining evidence of Han wooden architecture is a collection of scattered ceramic roof tiles.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199976-381)[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang19821–40-382) The oldest surviving wooden halls in China date to the Tang dynasty.[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinhardt2004228–238-383) Architectural historian Robert L. Thorp points out the scarcity of Han-era archaeological remains, and claims that often unreliable Han-era literary and artistic sources are used by historians as clues concerning lost Han architecture.[[381]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThorp1986360–378-384)

Though Han wooden structures decayed, some Han dynasty ruins made of brick, stone, and [rammed earth](/wiki/Rammed_earth "Rammed earth") remain intact. This includes stone pillar-gates, brick tomb chambers, rammed-earth [city walls](/wiki/Chinese_city_wall "Chinese city wall"), rammed-earth and brick [beacon](/wiki/Beacon "Beacon") towers, rammed-earth sections of the [Great Wall](/wiki/Great_Wall "Great Wall"), rammed-earth platforms where elevated halls once stood, and two rammed-earth castles in [Gansu](/wiki/Gansu "Gansu").[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang19821,_30,_39–40,_148–149-385)[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChang200791–92-386)[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMortonLewis200556-387)[[d]](#cite_note-388) The ruins of rammed-earth walls that once surrounded the capitals Chang'an and Luoyang still stand, along with their [drainage systems](/wiki/Sewage_collection_and_disposal "Sewage collection and disposal") of brick arches, ditches, and ceramic [water pipes](/wiki/Water_pipe "Water pipe").[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang19821–39-389) Monumental stone pillar-gates called *[que](/wiki/Que_(tower) "Que (tower)")*, of which 29 dated to the Han survive, formed entrances of walled enclosures at shrine and tomb sites.[[386]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinhardt2005a279-390)[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiu200255-391) These pillars feature artistic imitations of wooden and ceramic building components such as roof tiles, eaves, and [balustrades](/wiki/Balustrades "Balustrades").[[388]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinhardt2005a279–280-392)[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiu200255-391)

The [courtyard house](/wiki/Courtyard_house "Courtyard house") is the most common type of home portrayed in Han artwork.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey199976-381) Ceramic architectural [models of buildings](/wiki/Architectural_model "Architectural model"), like houses and towers, were found in Han tombs, perhaps to provide lodging for the dead in the afterlife. These provide valuable clues about lost wooden architecture. The artistic designs found on ceramic roof tiles of tower models are in some cases exact matches to Han roof tiles found at archaeological sites.[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinhardt2005b283–284-393)

Over ten Han-era underground tombs have been found, many of them featuring archways, [vaulted](/wiki/Vaulted "Vaulted") chambers, and domed roofs.[[390]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1982175–178-394) Underground vaults and domes did not require buttress supports since they were held in place by earthen pits.[[391]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson2000108-395) The use of brick vaults and domes in aboveground Han structures is unknown.[[391]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson2000108-395)

From Han literary sources, it is known that wooden-trestle [beam bridges](/wiki/Beam_bridge "Beam bridge"), [arch bridges](/wiki/Arch_bridge "Arch bridge"), [simple suspension bridges](/wiki/Simple_suspension_bridge "Simple suspension bridge"), and floating [pontoon bridges](/wiki/Pontoon_bridge "Pontoon bridge") existed during the Han.[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986d161–188-396) However, there are only two known references to arch bridges in Han literature.[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c171–172-397) There is only one Han-era relief sculpture, located in Sichuan, that depicts an arch bridge.[[394]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiu200256-398)

Underground [mine shafts](/wiki/Mine_shafts "Mine shafts") were dug to extract metal ores, with some reaching depths of more than 100 m (330 ft).[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1968191–194-399)[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1982105-400) [Borehole](/wiki/Borehole "Borehole") drilling and [derricks](/wiki/Derrick "Derrick") were used to lift [brine](/wiki/Brine "Brine") to iron pans where it was distilled into salt. The distillation furnaces were heated by [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") funnelled to the surface through [bamboo pipelines](/wiki/Pipeline_transport "Pipeline transport").[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1968191–194-399)[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETom1989103-401)[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERonan199491-402) It is possible that these boreholes reached a total depth of 600 m (2,000 ft).[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1968193–194-403)

### Mechanical and hydraulic engineering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Mechanical and hydraulic engineering")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Winnowing_machine_and_tilt_hammer.JPG/250px-Winnowing_machine_and_tilt_hammer.JPG)](/wiki/File:Winnowing_machine_and_tilt_hammer.JPG)

A Han dynasty pottery model of two men operating a [winnowing machine](/wiki/Fengshanche "Fengshanche") with a [crank handle](/wiki/Crank_(mechanism) "Crank (mechanism)") and a [tilt hammer](/wiki/Tilt_hammer "Tilt hammer") used to pound grain

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/EastHanSeismograph.JPG/250px-EastHanSeismograph.JPG)](/wiki/File:EastHanSeismograph.JPG)

A modern replica of [Zhang Heng](/wiki/Zhang_Heng "Zhang Heng")'s [seismometer](/wiki/Seismometer "Seismometer")

Han-era mechanical engineering comes largely from the choice observational writings of sometimes-disinterested Confucian scholars who generally considered scientific and engineering endeavours to be far beneath them.[[400]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser2014370-404) Professional artisan-engineers (*jiang* 匠) did not leave behind detailed records of their work.[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c2,_9-405)[[e]](#cite_note-406) Han scholars, who often had little or no expertise in mechanical engineering, sometimes provided insufficient information on the various technologies they described.[[402]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c2-407)

Nevertheless, some literary sources provide crucial information. For example, in 15 BC the philosopher and poet [Yang Xiong](/wiki/Yang_Xiong_(author) "Yang Xiong (author)") described the invention of the [belt drive](/wiki/Belt_drive "Belt drive") for a [quilling](/wiki/Quilling "Quilling") machine, which was of great importance to early textile manufacturing.[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1988207–208-408) The inventions of mechanical engineer and craftsman [Ding Huan](/wiki/Ding_Huan "Ding Huan") are mentioned in the *Miscellaneous Notes on the Western Capital*.[[404]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarbieri-Low2007197-409) Around AD 180, Ding created a manually operated rotary fan used for air conditioning within palace buildings.[[405]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c99,_134,_151,_233-410) Ding also used [gimbals](/wiki/Gimbal "Gimbal") as pivotal supports for one of his incense burners and invented the world's first known [zoetrope](/wiki/Zoetrope "Zoetrope") lamp.[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986b123,_233–234-411)

Modern archaeology has led to the discovery of Han artwork portraying inventions which were otherwise absent in Han literary sources. As observed in Han miniature tomb models, but not in literary sources, the [crank handle](/wiki/Crank_(mechanism) "Crank (mechanism)") was used to operate the [fans](/wiki/Fan_(mechanical) "Fan (mechanical)") of [winnowing machines](/wiki/Fengshanche "Fengshanche") that separated grain from [chaff](/wiki/Chaff "Chaff").[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c116–119,_Plate_CLVI-412) The [odometer](/wiki/Odometer "Odometer") cart, invented during the Han period, measured journey lengths, using mechanical figures banging drums and gongs to indicate each distance travelled.[[408]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c281–285-413) This invention is depicted in Han artwork by the 2nd century, yet detailed written descriptions were not offered until the 3rd century.[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c283–285-414)

Modern archaeologists have also unearthed specimens of devices used during the Han dynasty, for example a pair of sliding metal [calipers](/wiki/Caliper "Caliper") used by craftsmen for making minute measurements. These calipers contain inscriptions of the exact day and year they were manufactured. These tools are not mentioned in any Han literary sources.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe1968195–196-415)

The [waterwheel](/wiki/Waterwheel "Waterwheel") appeared in Chinese records during the Han. As mentioned by Huan Tan c. 20 AD, they were used to turn gears that lifted iron [trip hammers](/wiki/Trip_hammer "Trip hammer"), and were used in pounding, [threshing](/wiki/Threshing "Threshing"), and polishing grain.[[411]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c183–184,_390–392-416) However, there is no sufficient evidence for the [watermill](/wiki/Watermill "Watermill") in China until around the 5th century.[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c396–400-417) The administrator, mechanical engineer, and metallurgist [Du Shi](/wiki/Du_Shi "Du Shi") (d. 38 AD) created a waterwheel-powered [reciprocator](/wiki/Reciprocating_motion "Reciprocating motion") that worked the [bellows](/wiki/Bellows "Bellows") for the smelting of iron.[[413]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007184-418)[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c370-419) Waterwheels were also used to power [chain pumps](/wiki/Chain_pump "Chain pump") that lifted water to raised irrigation ditches. The chain pump was first mentioned in China by the philosopher Wang Chong in his 1st-century *[Lunheng](/wiki/Lunheng "Lunheng")*.[[415]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c89,_110,_342–344-420)

The [armillary sphere](/wiki/Armillary_sphere "Armillary sphere"), a three-dimensional representation of the movements in the [celestial sphere](/wiki/Celestial_sphere "Celestial sphere"), was invented by the Han during the 1st century BC.[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a343-421) Using a [water clock](/wiki/Water_clock "Water clock"), waterwheel, and a series of gears, the Court Astronomer [Zhang Heng](/wiki/Zhang_Heng "Zhang Heng") (78–139 AD) was able to mechanically rotate his metal-ringed armillary sphere.[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071050-422)[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c30,_479_footnote_e-423)[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMortonLewis200570-424)[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowman2000595-425) To address the problem of slowed timekeeping in the [pressure head](/wiki/Pressure_head "Pressure head") of the inflow water clock, Zhang was the first in China to install an additional tank between the reservoir and inflow vessel.[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071050-422)[[421]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c479_footnote_e-426)

Zhang also invented a device he termed an "earthquake weathervane" (*houfeng didong yi* 候風地動儀), which the British sinologist and historian [Joseph Needham](/wiki/Joseph_Needham "Joseph Needham") described as "the ancestor of all [seismographs](/wiki/Seismographs "Seismographs")".[[422]](#cite_note-427) This device was able to detect the exact [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_direction "Cardinal direction") or ordinal direction of earthquakes from hundreds of kilometres away.[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071050-422)[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser2014375-428)[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMortonLewis200570-424) It employed an [inverted pendulum](/wiki/Inverted_pendulum "Inverted pendulum") that, when disturbed by ground tremors, would trigger a set of gears that dropped a metal ball from one of eight dragon mouths (representing all eight directions) into a metal toad's mouth.[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a626–631-429) The account of this device in the *[Book of the Later Han](/wiki/Book_of_the_Later_Han "Book of the Later Han")* describes how, on one occasion, one of the metal balls was triggered without any of the observers feeling a disturbance. Several days later, a messenger arrived bearing news that an earthquake had struck in Longxi Commandery (modern Gansu), the direction the device had indicated, which forced the officials at court to admit the efficacy of Zhang's device.[[425]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser2014376-430)

### Mathematics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Mathematics")]

Further information: [Chinese mathematics § Han mathematics](/wiki/Chinese_mathematics#Han_mathematics "Chinese mathematics")

Three Han mathematical treatises still exist. These are the *[Book on Numbers and Computation](/wiki/Book_on_Numbers_and_Computation "Book on Numbers and Computation")*, the *[Zhoubi Suanjing](/wiki/Zhoubi_Suanjing "Zhoubi Suanjing"),* and the *[Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art](/wiki/Nine_Chapters_on_the_Mathematical_Art "Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art")*. Han mathematical achievements include solving problems with right triangles, [square roots](/wiki/Square_root "Square root"), [cube roots](/wiki/Cube_root "Cube root"), and [matrix methods](/wiki/Matrix_(mathematics) "Matrix (mathematics)"),[[426]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDauben2007212-431)[[427]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiuFengJiangZheng20039–10-432) finding more accurate [approximations for pi](/wiki/Chronology_of_computation_of_%CF%80 "Chronology of computation of π"),[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a99–100-433)[[429]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerggrenBorweinBorwein200427-434) providing [mathematical proof](/wiki/Mathematical_proof "Mathematical proof") of the [Pythagorean theorem](/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem "Pythagorean theorem"),[[430]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDauben2007219–222-435)[[431]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a22-436) use of the [decimal fraction](/wiki/Fraction_(mathematics) "Fraction (mathematics)"),[[432]](#cite_note-437) [Gaussian elimination](/wiki/Gaussian_elimination "Gaussian elimination") to solve [linear equations](/wiki/System_of_linear_equations "System of linear equations"),[[433]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShenCrossleyLun1999388-438)[[434]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStraffin1998166-439)[[435]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a24–25,_121-440) and [continued fractions](/wiki/Continued_fraction "Continued fraction") to find the [roots of equations](/wiki/Root_of_a_function "Root of a function").[[436]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a65–66-441)

One of the Han's greatest mathematical advancements was the world's first use of [negative numbers](/wiki/Negative_number "Negative number"). Negative numbers first appeared in the *Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art* as black [counting rods](/wiki/Counting_rods "Counting rods"), where positive numbers were represented by red counting rods.[[427]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiuFengJiangZheng20039–10-432) Negative numbers were also used by the Greek mathematician [Diophantus](/wiki/Diophantus "Diophantus") around AD 275, and in the 7th-century [Bakhshali manuscript](/wiki/Bakhshali_manuscript "Bakhshali manuscript") of [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara"), South Asia,[[437]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETeresi200265–66-442) but were not widely accepted in Europe until the 16th century.[[427]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiuFengJiangZheng20039–10-432)

The Han applied mathematics to various diverse disciplines. In [musical tuning](/wiki/Musical_tuning "Musical tuning"), [Jing Fang](/wiki/Jing_Fang "Jing Fang") (78–37 BC) realized that 53 [perfect fifths](/wiki/Perfect_fifth "Perfect fifth") was approximate to 31 [octaves](/wiki/Octave "Octave"). He also created a [musical scale](/wiki/Musical_scale "Musical scale") of 60 tones, calculating the difference at 177147⁄176776 (the same value of [53 equal temperament](/wiki/53_equal_temperament "53 equal temperament") discovered by the German mathematician [Nicholas Mercator](/wiki/Nicholas_Mercator "Nicholas Mercator") [1620–1687], i.e. 353/284).[[438]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcClainMing1979212-443)[[439]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986b218–219-444)

### Astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Astronomy")]

Further information: [Chinese astronomy](/wiki/Chinese_astronomy "Chinese astronomy")

Mathematics were essential in drafting the [astronomical calendar](/wiki/Chinese_calendar "Chinese calendar"), a [lunisolar calendar](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar") that used the Sun and Moon as time-markers throughout the year.[[440]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECullen20067-445)[[441]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELloyd1996168-446) In the 5th century BC, during the [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period"), the Chinese established the Sifen calendar (古四分歷), which measured the [tropical year](/wiki/Tropical_year "Tropical year") at 365.25 days. This was replaced in 104 BC with the Taichu calendar (太初曆) that measured the tropical year at 365+385⁄1539 (~ 365.25016) days and the [lunar month](/wiki/Lunar_month "Lunar month") at 29+43⁄81 days.[[442]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeng200567-447) However, Emperor Zhang later reinstated the Sifen calendar.[[443]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007498-448)

Han dynasty astronomers made [star catalogues](/wiki/Star_catalogue "Star catalogue") and detailed records of comets that appeared in the night sky, including recording the appearance of the comet now known as [Halley's Comet](/wiki/Halley%27s_Comet "Halley's Comet") in 12 BC.[[444]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe199461,_69-449)[[445]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi2006173–175-450)[[446]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESunKistemaker19975,_21–23-451)[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalchin200327-452) They adopted a [geocentric model](/wiki/Geocentric_model "Geocentric model") of the universe, theorizing that it was a sphere surrounding the Earth in the centre.[[448]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDauben2007214-453)[[449]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESunKistemaker199762-454)[[450]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuang198864-455) They assumed that the Sun, Moon, and planets were spherical and not disc-shaped. They also thought that the illumination of the Moon and planets was caused by sunlight, that [lunar eclipses](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse") occurred when the Earth obstructed sunlight falling onto the Moon, and that a [solar eclipse](/wiki/Solar_eclipse "Solar eclipse") occurred when the Moon obstructed sunlight from reaching the Earth.[[451]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a227,_414-456) Although others disagreed with his model, Wang Chong accurately described the [water cycle](/wiki/Water_cycle "Water cycle") of the evaporation of water into clouds.[[452]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a468-457)

### Cartography, ships, and vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Cartography, ships, and vehicles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Eastern_Han_pottery_boat.JPG/250px-Eastern_Han_pottery_boat.JPG)](/wiki/File:Eastern_Han_pottery_boat.JPG)

An Eastern Han pottery boat model with a steering [rudder](/wiki/Rudder "Rudder") at the stern and anchor at the bow

Both literary and archaeological evidence has demonstrated that [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") in China predated the Han.[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHsu199390–93-458)[[454]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a534–535-459) Some of the oldest Han-era maps that have been discovered were written using ink on silk, and were found amongst the [Mawangdui Silk Texts](/wiki/Mawangdui_Silk_Texts "Mawangdui Silk Texts") in a 2nd-century BC tomb in Hunan.[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHsu199390–93-458)[[455]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansen2000125-460) The general [Ma Yuan](/wiki/Ma_Yuan_(Han_dynasty) "Ma Yuan (Han dynasty)") created the world's first known [raised-relief map](/wiki/Raised-relief_map "Raised-relief map") from rice in the 1st century.[[456]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007659-461) This date could be revised if the tomb of [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") is excavated and the *Shiji*'s account of a model map of the empire is proven to be true.[[457]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a580–581-462)

Although the use of [graduated scales](/wiki/Scale_(map) "Scale (map)") and [grid references](/wiki/Grid_reference "Grid reference") in maps was not thoroughly described prior to the work of [Pei Xiu](/wiki/Pei_Xiu "Pei Xiu") (AD 224–271), there is evidence that their use was introduced in the early 2nd century by the cartographer Zhang Heng.[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071050-422)[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHsu199390–93-458)[[458]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986a538–540-463)[[459]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1974359-464)

The Han sailed in various types of ships that differed from those used in previous eras, such as the [tower ship](/wiki/Tower_ship "Tower ship"). The [*junk* design](/wiki/Junk_(ship) "Junk (ship)") was developed and realized during the Han era. Junk ships featured a square-ended [bow](/wiki/Bow_(watercraft) "Bow (watercraft)") and [stern](/wiki/Stern "Stern"), a flat-bottomed [hull](/wiki/Hull_(watercraft) "Hull (watercraft)") or [carvel-shaped](/wiki/Carvel_(boat_building) "Carvel (boat building)") hull with no [keel](/wiki/Keel "Keel") or [sternpost](/wiki/Sternpost "Sternpost"), and [solid transverse bulkheads](/wiki/Bulkhead_(partition) "Bulkhead (partition)") in the place of [structural ribs found in Western vessels.[[460]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurnbull200214-465)[[461]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986d390–391-466) Moreover, Han ships were the first in the world to be steered using a [rudder](/wiki/Rudder "Rudder") at the stern, in contrast to the simpler [steering oar](/wiki/Steering_oar "Steering oar") used for riverine transport, allowing them to sail on the high seas.[[462]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986d627–628-467)[[463]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChung2005152-468)[[464]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETom1989103–104-469)[[465]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdshead2000156-470)[[466]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFairbankGoldman199893-471)[[467]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlock200393,_123-472)

Although ox carts and chariots were previously used in China, the wheelbarrow was first used in Han China in the 1st century BC.[[468]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c263–267-473)[[469]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenberger200613-474) Han artwork of horse-drawn chariots shows that the Warring-States-Era heavy wooden yoke placed around a horse's chest was replaced by the softer *breast strap*.[[470]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c308–312,_319–323-475) Later, during the [Northern Wei](/wiki/Northern_Wei "Northern Wei") (386–534), the fully developed [horse collar](/wiki/Horse_collar "Horse collar") was invented.[[470]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham1986c308–312,_319–323-475)

### Medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_dynasty&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Medicine")]

Further information: [Traditional Chinese medicine § Han dynasty](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_medicine#Han_dynasty "Traditional Chinese medicine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Qigong_taiji_meditation.jpg/250px-Qigong_taiji_meditation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Qigong_taiji_meditation.jpg)

The physical exercise chart; a [painting on silk](/wiki/Mawangdui_silk_texts "Mawangdui silk texts") depicting the practice of *[Daoyin](/wiki/Daoyin "Daoyin")* – Mawangdui tomb No. 3

Han-era medical physicians believed that the human body was subject to the same forces of nature that governed the greater universe, namely the [cosmological](/wiki/Cosmological "Cosmological") cycles of yin and yang and the [five phases](/wiki/Five_phases "Five phases"). Each organ of the body was associated with a particular phase. Illness was viewed as a sign that [qi](/wiki/Qi "Qi"), or vital energy, channels leading to a certain organ had been disrupted. Thus, Han-era physicians prescribed medicine that was believed to counteract this imbalance.[[471]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi2006181–182-476)[[472]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESunKistemaker19973–4-477)[[473]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHsu200175-478)

For example, since the wood phase was believed to promote the fire phase, medicinal ingredients associated with the wood phase could be used to heal an organ associated with the fire phase.[[471]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECsikszentmihalyi2006181–182-476) Besides dieting, Han physicians also prescribed [moxibustion](/wiki/Moxibustion "Moxibustion"), [acupuncture](/wiki/Acupuncture "Acupuncture"), and [callisthenics](/wiki/Callisthenics "Callisthenics") as methods of maintaining one's health.[[474]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007332-479)[[475]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOmura200315,_19–22-480)[[476]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoewe199465-481)[[477]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELo200123-482) When surgery was performed by the Chinese physician [Hua Tuo](/wiki/Hua_Tuo "Hua Tuo") (d. AD 208), he used [anaesthesia](/wiki/Anaesthesia "Anaesthesia") to numb his patients' pain and prescribed a rubbing ointment that allegedly sped up the healing process for surgical wounds.[[474]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny2007332-479) The physician [Zhang Zhongjing](/wiki/Zhang_Zhongjing "Zhang Zhongjing") (c. 150 – c. 219 AD) is known to have written the *[Shanghan Lun](/wiki/Shanghan_Lun "Shanghan Lun")* ("Dissertation on Typhoid Fever"), and it is thought that he and Hua Tuo collaborated to compile the *[Shennong Bencaojing](/wiki/Shennong_Bencaojing "Shennong Bencaojing")* medical text.[[478]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Crespigny20071055-483)
